entry.title: Leading the Way for Post-Quantum Cryptography
entry.content:
By Sandip Dholakia, Chair Cryptography Innovation Hub, and Volkmar Lotz, Project Lead Crypto Agility Initiative, SAP


Quantum technologies are advancing rapidly, leading to a cryptographically relevant quantum computer that can break today’s public-key cryptography, which will be available sooner rather than later.

Like major industry peers, SAP is aware of the increased risk and is striving to be post-quantum ready by 2029 across its products, services and infrastructure. SAP has established a Cryptography Center or Excellence (CoE) that governs the migration process, maintains a cryptographic inventory, and coordinates migration projects across the company following a roadmap recognizing technical and business priorities for the identified cryptographic assets. Protecting customer data and privacy remains SAP’s highest priority, and to support this commitment, the SAP cryptography CoE has adopted a long-term strategy focused on cryptographic agility rather than one-off migration projects.

 

The Risk

Quantum computers can solve certain mathematical problems faster than classical computers. In 1994, Peter Shor demonstrated that a sufficiently powerful quantum computer could efficiently factor large integers and solve discrete logarithm problems. This breakthrough directly threatens widely used public-key cryptography algorithms, including RSA, Diffie-Hellman, and Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC) as their security relies on the difficulty of solving these problems with a classical computer. These algorithms form the foundation of many security mechanisms used across the industry, including within SAP products.


Why the Timeline Matters

Although the cybersecurity professionals understood the threat for decades, momentum accelerated after the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) proposed a timeline in 2024 to introduce Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) alongside traditional cryptography, starting in 2030 and fully phasing out traditional cryptography by 2035. This timeline is challenged by recent advances in quantum technologies: accelerated hardware development, improved quantum error correction and optimizations for Shor’s algorithm suggest that the gap between existing quantum computers and those needed to break public-key cryptography is closing. Considering these developments, SAP has raised the ambition to replace the current algorithms by quantum-resilient ones across its products, services and infrastructures by 2029.

This reflects an industry trend: Earlier in 2026, major industry peers, Google and Microsoft among them, announced plans to complete their PQC migration by 2029. In June 2026, the White House issued Executive Order 14412, requiring High Value Assets (HVAs) within U.S. federal agencies to be quantum-safe by the end of 2030.


The Challenge

History shows that replacing cryptographic standards can take years, sometimes decades. Cryptographic protocols are deeply embedded in applications, infrastructure, communications, and operational processes. Even small changes can have far-reaching and unintended consequences. As a result, organizations often hesitate to take immediate action to perform necessary upgrades, increasing exposure to evolving threats.

SAP leverages cryptographic agility to implement a sustainable approach to meet evolving cryptographic requirements.


Cryptographic Agility at SAP

SAP’s strategy is built around cryptographic agility—the ability to adapt quickly to future cryptographic changes without major disruption. SAP is implementing a phased enterprise-wide approach:

• Governance: The Cryptography CoE provides executive oversight, establishes milestones, defines success metrics, and guides product teams.
• Inventory: Teams identify cryptographic assets and generate Cryptographic Bills of Materials (CBoMs) using industry-standard tools and CycloneDX formats.
• Prioritization: Assets are ranked based on criticality to guide migration planning. This takes into account that some types of attacks can be launched already today, for instance, collecting encrypted data in transit for later decryption with a quantum computer (“harvest now, decrypt later”)
• Implementation: PQC algorithms are introduced in phases, following established roadmaps reflecting the prioritization.
• Validation: New implementations undergo rigorous testing before production deployment.

Over time, these capabilities will become automated and embedded into SAP’s Secure Software Development and Operations Lifecycle (SecureSDOL).


SAP’s Position

• Taking recent developments in quantum technology into account, SAP strives to become post-quantum cryptography ready by 2029.
• SAP is actively advancing toward quantum resistance and aligning with emerging regulatory and industry expectations. The company’s primary focus is on adopting NIST-recommended PQC algorithms while remaining open to other industry-proven solutions where appropriate.
• SAP has already released an updated Common Cryptographic Library (v8.6.4) that includes PQC capabilities for product teams. At present, SAP does not anticipate significant performance or cost impacts for customers, partners, or vendors during the migration.

SAP’s objective is: protect products, services, and customer data against evolving threats while delivering secure, resilient, and future-ready solutions in the post-quantum era.

entry.title: What We Learned While Building an AI Center of Excellence
entry.content:
Introduction

In recent months, artificial intelligence has been one of the most frequent topics of conversation during client and partnership meetings, and among technology peers, all trying to figure out what to do with it. It is natural that there is such interest. With the emergence of new AI models and copilots, and the fast appearance of automation systems, all organizations try to understand how these technologies can transform their businesses for the better. We decided to set up an AI Center of Excellence, like many other technology companies looking into AI and wondering how to integrate it into their offerings and develop the internal capabilities to do so.

Initially, we thought the discussion would be primarily about technology. We expected to spend most of our time evaluating platforms, models, and emerging tools, and determining how they could help solve specific business challenges. After all, AI was not new to us. We had already been delivering AI-powered solutions across different industries for years, so how difficult could it be to just make some adjustments in our proposal?

What wasn't immediately clear was what an AI Center of Excellence would add to capabilities we already had. If we were already delivering AI projects and generating business outcomes, what exactly was missing?As we dug deeper into the idea, it became clear that the answer had very little to do with technology. There were four main things that needed to be addressed in order to make an impact: Knowledge Management, Governance, Prioritization of Opportunities, and creating a framework to scale AI solutions. This was a major turning point for us going forward.

For us, an AI Center of Excellence is not a separate group of AI specialists working in isolation. It is a structure designed to connect expertise, capture lessons learned, establish common practices, and help scale successful approaches across projects and teams.

 

The First Realization: We Didn't Have an AI Problem

The very first thing we learned was that this was not really an AI issue at all, it was a capability problem. We had done a number of projects involving intelligent document processing, forecasting, automation, and enterprise level AI. The skills and experience were there, the use cases were there, and we had a number of customers who were benefiting from our efforts.

What we soon began to realize, however, was that doing well does not mean you are building capability. A lot of the lessons learned were specific to the engagement, the team, or the project. A team working on a document processing solution might develop an effective approach for handling unstructured data, while another team building a forecasting model could solve similar challenges around data preparation, governance, or user adoption. The knowledge existed within the organization, but there was no consistent mechanism for capturing those insights and making them available across future projects.

As our interest and involvement with AI expanded, it quickly became apparent that the real issue here was not about coming up with more uses for AI. It was about developing a structure that let us build on what we were learning. 

 

The Second Realization: AI Projects Don't Automatically Become AI Capabilities

 

We recognized a difference between being good at completing projects successfully and being an organization capable of using artificial intelligence. Completing a successful engagement and creating an AI solution that addresses a particular business problem brings great value for a company. However, the benefits of that solution stay with the project team, and once the work is done, any knowledge or assets built along the way will simply go to waste.

This is where a Center of Excellence starts to make sense. In some way, you have to ensure that the experience gained from one initiative can be applied to the next one. It is about more than just doing engagements and projects, because such an approach leads to wasting resources. A Center of Excellence allows organizations to benefit from experience gained, to establish processes for dealing with situations, and create resources that can be reused. This enables organizations to respond immediately without having to start from scratch each time an AI-driven solution is required to solve a specific business challenge.

For example, a governance framework developed for an intelligent document processing initiative or a set of integration patterns used to connect AI services with SAP business processes can often be adapted for future projects. Teams no longer need to start from scratch every time a new opportunity emerges. The goal is not simply to deliver more AI projects. The goal is to create a foundation that enables teams to apply what they have already learned, reduce duplication of effort, and consistently deliver value across different business scenarios. 

The Third Realization: Most AI Challenges Are Operational

One of the greatest surprises throughout this journey was that discussions involving AI always seemed to come to more typical problems in business. Discussions that started with AI assistant tools, automation, or generative AI, the conversation often shifted to focus on whether business processes and underlying data were actually prepared to support these technologies. In many instances, the problem was not the technology itself. Instead, it was the preparation required to enable it.

 

It is a particularly important consideration when considering AI applications within SAP landscapes. The power of SAP solutions is heavily dependent on the quality and accessibility of enterprise data, which means that the ability to connect innovative possibilities with well-defined business processes and reliable data is essential for any sustained benefit. In developing the vision for the AI Center of Excellence, it became clear that the function goes beyond the promotion of AI and is also instrumental in enabling AI adoption.

Final Thoughts

When we first began researching the concept of a Center of Excellence for AI, it was clear to us that the discussion would focus on technology. What we found was an entirely different story. The research made us rethink our views on knowledge exchange, replicating best practices, and fostering a culture in which an organization could leverage its investments in AI to drive consistent value creation.

Maybe the key lesson learned from this experience was the understanding gained regarding the transition from one-off AI projects to a sustained AI capability. AI technology will continue to evolve, and new opportunities will continue to emerge. Organizations that benefit most from those advances may not be the ones with access to the most advanced tools. What matters is their ability to capture knowledge, apply successful practices, and scale them across the organization. When we began this process, our intention was to build an AI capability. What we really did was lay the groundwork for future AI capabilities to thrive.

entry.title: FI Documents Missing an ODN? The ODN Insert Report Fixes That in One Run
entry.content:
If Official Document Numbering (ODN) has been activated, there may be Financial Accounting (FI) documents that were posted before ODN was introduced and therefore do not contain an ODN number. In addition, some FI-relevant posting processes may not currently be integrated with the standard ODN framework, such as postings originating from Flexible Real Estate Management (RE-FX) or other applications that create FI documents through interfaces not covered by ODN.

The ODN Insert for Accounting Documents report (transaction ODN_FI_INV_INSERT) addresses these scenarios by allowing an ODN value and legal timestamp to be added to FI documents that have already been posted. The report can be used for individual documents or for larger volumes through a mass upload process, regardless of the originating application or posting source.

Scope Note

Scope Note: This report applies to SAP S/4HANA (on-premise) and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. It ships as part of the H1/2026 FI ODN framework enhancements together with the credit-memo / reversal / BAdI functionality. Availability starts at 2025 FPS02 and is back-ported to earlier releases via SAP Note 3739128. A prerequisite chain of seven additional SAP Notes must be applied in a specific order — see the Note Sequence section below.

Main Insight

The ODN Insert for Accounting Documents report closes a real operational gap. Whenever an FI document exists in the ledger without a matching entry in table GSLOG_LFD — or with an entry whose ODN value is empty — you now have a governed way to fix it without touching the accounting document itself.

Two things happen in one shot:

  1. The report writes (or overwrites an empty value) the ODN value on the accounting document.
  2. It writes (or overwrites an empty value) the ODN legal timestamp.

Two ways to run it:

  • Single-document mode — enter one document number, one company code, one fiscal year, one ODN Type, one ODN Value and one ODN Country; simulate; then commit.
  • Mass-upload mode — provide a CSV file with many document keys and the target ODN values. SAP Note 3739128 ships the file template.

The same report handles both customer and supplier invoice scenarios in Financial Accounting, and it applies whether the document was posted through FB01, FB70, FB60 (if ODN Value was left empty), FV70 + FBV0 (parked → posted), or any other FI entry point.

Two guardrails protect the process:

  • Users must be assigned to authorization object LFD_ODNREP to run Simulation and Update.
  • Only one ODN country per document — either the company-code country or the reporting country.

Where You'll See It in the System

1. The Selection Screen — Choose Your Target

The report entry screen lets you enter a single document (company code + document number + fiscal year) or upload a file for mass processing. You also select whether you want to Simulate or Update:

2. The Result — Traceable, Row by Row

After the run, every processed document is listed with its outcome — inserted, overwritten (only for empty records), or skipped with a reason. This log is the audit trail you'll hand to your compliance team:

3. Verification in FB03 — The ODN Is Now on the Document

Open the accounting document in FB03 and the ODN value now appears exactly where it would have if it had been captured at posting time (for system availability see SAP Note 3716857)

4. Additional Fiscal Data Tab — The Full Picture

On the Additional Fiscal Data tab of the same document, you see the full triple: ODN country, ODN value, and ODN legal timestamp — all three now maintained:

Two Typical Scenarios

Example 1 — Post-Enablement Backfill: You posted an FI invoicing document via transaction FB01 (Post Document) before ODN was enabled for that company code. The document has no ODN entry in GSLOG_LFD at all. Run the report in single-document or mass-upload mode to insert both the ODN value and the ODN legal timestamp — no reversal, no re-post.

Example 2 — Parked Document Cleanup: You created a parked customer invoice via transaction FV70 (Park Customer Invoice) and later approved it with FBV0 (Post Parked Document). Run the report against that single document to write the correct number and timestamp in place.

The BAdI Is Included

An easy misconception: "if the report writes directly to the tables, my custom numbering rules won't apply." Wrong. The ODN Insert report calls the BADI_FI_AD_ODN_MODIFY BAdI on every processed document — the same BAdI that fires during FB70 / FB75 / FB60 / FB65 entry, during FB08 reversals, and during BAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST calls.

That means:

  • If you've implemented country-specific numbering logic in the BAdI, it runs automatically.

📖See the BAdI Help Portal page for the method signature and implementation guidance.

Note Sequence — Apply These in Order

If you're on a release below 2025 FPS02, implement the following SAP Notes step-by-step, in the exact sequence shown. Skipping the order can break dependencies between the prerequisite objects:

  1. 3708326 — FI ODN - Prerequisite Objects, BAdI (Manual Input Scenarios)
  2. 3729848 — FI ODN - Prerequisite Objects, Supplier Invoice Class III
  3. 3720120 — FI ODN - Prerequisite Objects, Customer Invoice Class III
  4. 3739126 — FI ODN - Prerequisite Objects, Update Service Class II
  5. 3739128 — FI ODN - ODN Insert for Accounting Documents (the report itself)
  6. 3776153 — FI ODN - Prerequisite Objects, Class for BTE Events
  7. 3742537 — FI ODN - BAdI in Accounting Transactions; BAPI and Reversal Enablers
  8. 3742475 — FI ODN - FI ODN functions available for transactions FB65 and FB75

Only after all eight notes are applied is the retrofit report fully wired to the rest of the FI ODN framework (BAdI, reversals, credit memos). If you're already on 2025 FPS02 or higher, the whole set is delivered with the release — no action needed.

Practical Takeaway

To adopt the ODN Insert report:

  • Confirm your release: 2025 FPS02 or newer, or an earlier release with the eight prerequisite SAP Notes applied in order
  • Run the customizing for your countries and processes in scope
  • Assign authorization object LFD_ODNREP to the users who will run Simulation and Update — and scope it to the ODN processes they're allowed to touch
  • Download the mass-upload file template attached to SAP Note 3739128
  • Run a Simulation first — on a single document, then on a representative mass-upload batch — and inspect the result list
  • Verify a handful of documents in FB03 (both the header and the Additional Fiscal Data tab) or via CDS view I_ACCOUNTINGDOCUMENTODN before scaling to production volumes
  • Coordinate the run with your compliance / audit team — the ODN legal timestamp is the value they'll ask about

Summary

The ODN Insert for Accounting Documents report (transaction ODN_FI_INV_INSERT) is the missing puzzle piece that lets you retrofit Official Document Numbers onto already-posted FI documents — either one at a time or thousands at once via mass upload (foreground processing: maximum 1,000 records; background processing: maximum 5,000 records). It calls the same BADI_FI_AD_ODN_MODIFY BAdI as every other FI ODN entry point, so your custom numbering logic applies automatically. Combined with the new FB08 reversal and FB65/FB75 credit-memo support, the FI ODN framework now covers a powerful scope.

Key Takeaways

  • Retrofit without reversal. Report ODN_FI_INV_INSERT writes ODN values and legal timestamps directly into already-posted FI documents — no reversal, no re-post, no modification of standard code.
  • Single or mass. Process one document interactively or upload a CSV file for bulk backfill; SAP Note 3739128 ships the file template.
  • The BAdI still fires. BADI_FI_AD_ODN_MODIFY is called during the retrofit run too — your country-specific numbering logic applies automatically, whether the document was posted yesterday or last year.
  • Authorization-controlled. Users need authorization object LFD_ODNREP to run Simulation and Update; scope it to the specific ODN processes they're allowed to touch.
  • One country per document. Either the company-code country or the reporting country.
  • Part of a bigger toolset. The retrofit report pairs with FB08 (reversals), FB65 (supplier credit memos), FB75 (customer credit memos), and the FI BAdI to give you complete FI ODN coverage.

Restrictions

  • The documents need to fit into the criteria to use one of the available ODN processes.
  • When the process is originated in logistics (cases of FI documents generated from sales or MM) this report inserts ODN only into the FI document.
  • Documents posted from modules for which there is not yet ODN support (such as Settlement Management) may fall back to FI ODN processes. It is advisable to use the report to cover these cases only if the ODN types can be customized for the FI processes consistently with the potential source processes.
  • In releases 103 and 102 there is currently no support for FB60 / FB65, FB70 / FB75, FB02 and FB03.
  • The reversal and BAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST are enabled with simple ODN creation, meaning the ODN values are proposed initial, and the customer may adapt them using the BAdI.

More Information

  • Insertion of ODN in Accounting Documents — SAP Help Portal
  • BAdI: Modify ODN Value and Legal Timestamp for Scenarios in Financial Accounting (BADI_FI_AD_ODN_MODIFY) — SAP Help Portal
  • SAP Notes (in application order): 3708326, 3729848, 3720120, 3739126, 3739128, 3776153, 3742537, 3742475

entry.title: Official Document Numbering Framework Extended: Credit Memos, Reversals, and a New BAdI
entry.content:
Hook

Until now, the Official Document Numbering (ODN) framework in SAP S/4HANA didn't cover credit memos or reversal documents in Financial Accounting. That has changed: the framework has been extended to support both scenarios natively, and a new BAdI gives you upgrade-safe control over ODN values across every FI entry point.

Scope Note

Scope Note: These enhancements apply to SAP S/4HANA (on-premise) and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. Availability starts at 2025 FPS02, and the features are back-ported down to 2023 SPS06, 2022 SPS08, 2021 SPS10, 2020 SPS12, and 1909 SPS14 via SAP Notes 3739128, 3742537, and 3742475. If you're on one of the listed support packages or higher, you don't need to implement the notes — the functionality is already there.

Main Insight

The Official Document Numbering framework in SAP S/4HANA has been extended in three directions at once, and together they close gaps for Financial Accounting.

First, you can now maintain the ODN value and ODN legal timestamp directly on customer credit memos (transaction FB75) and supplier credit memos (transaction FB65) — the same way you already could on invoices. No more workarounds, no more manual post-fix reports.

Second, the same maintenance is available on document reversals. After the reversal is posted, the ODN value can be maintained via transaction FB02 on the reversal document.

Third, and most powerful for anyone with custom fiscal rules: a new BAdI — BADI_FI_AD_ODN_MODIFY — lets you programmatically override the ODN value and legal timestamp for any FI scenario. Derive it from another SAP document, pull it from an external tax portal, or apply your own numbering logic — all without modifying standard code.

Where You'll See It in the System

Credit Memos (FB65 / FB75)

The ODN and legal timestamp fields appear on the Additional Fiscal Data tab of the credit memo entry screen, exactly where you'd expect them alongside the invoice fields:

Document Reversals (FB08)

When you reverse an FI document, the ODN value can be displayed/maintained via transactions FB03/FB02 on the reversal document:

The BAdI: When Standard Isn't Enough

The BADI_FI_AD_ODN_MODIFY BAdI exposes a single method — CHANGE_USER_INPUT_ODN — that fires in every relevant FI scenario:

  • Customer Invoice (FB70) and Customer Credit Memo (FB75)
  • Supplier Invoice (FB60) and Supplier Credit Memo (FB65)
  • Change and Display Documents (FB02, FB03)
  • FI Document Reversal (FB08)
  • The new ODN Insert report (transaction ODN_FI_INV_INSERT), for overwriting empty records and creating new ones
  • BAPI_ACC_DOCUMENT_POST (so RFC and IDoc flows are covered too)

Because the same BAdI covers all entry points, you write your logic once and it applies consistently — user entry, batch inserts, and API-driven postings all take the same path.

🔦 Spotlight: Retrofitting Historical Documents

Retrofit is possible. Report ODN_FI_INV_INSERT handles historical accounting documents that were posted before ODN was enabled — or that are missing values today. It can both overwrite empty records and create fresh ODN entries, and it uses the same BADI_FI_AD_ODN_MODIFY BAdI, so any custom logic you already implemented applies to the retrofit run as well.

This is a topic in its own right — configuration, selection variants, dry-run vs. commit, and audit considerations. A dedicated deep-dive blog is now available.

📖 Read the deep dive: FI Documents Missing an ODN? The ODN Insert Report Fixes That in One Run →

Practical Takeaway

To adopt the enhancement:

  • Confirm your release: 2025 FPS02, or one of the listed support packages with SAP Notes 3739128, 3742537, 3742475 applied
  • Enable ODN for the accounting document types used for credit memos and reversals (same customizing path you used for invoices)
  • Test end-to-end in FB65 and FB75 — the fields should now be visible and editable
  • For reversals, this can be done via FB02
  • If you have custom fiscal numbering logic, implement BADI_FI_AD_ODN_MODIFY instead of user exits or modifications
  • For historical documents missing an ODN, run transaction ODN_FI_INV_INSERT to fill in the gaps

Summary

The Official Document Numbering framework in SAP S/4HANA now covers the full FI document lifecycle: invoices, credit memos, and reversals — with a new BAdI that gives you upgrade-safe control when your country's rules go beyond the standard.

Key Takeaways

  • Full coverage for credit memos. FB65 and FB75 now expose ODN and legal timestamp fields natively — no more custom workarounds for supplier and customer credit memos.
  • Reversals are no longer a blind spot. FB08 lets you enter a distinct ODN and legal timestamp for the reversal document, which is essential in sequential-numbering jurisdictions.
  • One BAdI, all entry points. BADI_FI_AD_ODN_MODIFY intercepts user entry, batch runs, and BAPI-driven postings — write your logic once, keep it upgrade-safe.
  • Retrofit is possible. Report ODN_FI_INV_INSERT handles historical accounting documents that were posted before ODN was enabled or that are missing values. (See the dedicated deep-dive blog for configuration, selection variants, and audit considerations.)

More Information

  • Maintain User Input Fields of Official Document Numbering Based On Additional Fiscal Data — SAP Help Portal
  • BAdI: Modify ODN Value and Legal Timestamp for Scenarios in Financial Accounting (BADI_FI_AD_ODN_MODIFY) — SAP Help Portal
  • SAP Notes: 3739128, 3742537, 3742475

entry.title: SAP EWM: коли стандартного управління складом уже недостатньо
entry.content:
Управління складом у більшості компаній починається з базових операцій: прийом, зберігання, відвантаження. Для цього вистачає стандартного SAP WM (Warehouse Management) або навіть паперового обліку. Але коли масштаб зростає, з'являється SAP Extended Warehouse Management - рішення для складів нового рівня.

Що таке SAP EWM і чим він відрізняється від WM

SAP WM - класичний модуль управління складом, вбудований у SAP ECC та S/4HANA. Він справляється з базовими задачами, але має обмеження: немає підтримки складних стратегій розміщення, обмежені можливості для автоматизації, немає інтеграції з роботизованими системами.

SAP EWM (Extended Warehouse Management) - це окрема, значно потужніша система для управління складською логістикою, яка може розгортатися як вбудований у S/4HANA або як автономне рішення.

Що вміє SAP EWM, чого не вміє WM

•       Складні стратегії путвеводителів і розміщення - система сама визначає оптимальну комірку для кожного товару

•       Управління ресурсами складу - планування навантаження комірників, балансування потоків

•       Labor Management - нормування праці, аналіз продуктивності персоналу

•       Yard Management - управління двором: черги вантажівок, доки, розклад

•       Інтеграція з автоматизацією - конвеєри, роботи, ASRS (автоматизовані системи зберігання)

•       RF-Framework - робота зі сканерами та мобільними пристроями в режимі реального часу

Вбудований EWM vs автономний EWM

З виходом S/4HANA 1709 SAP почав пропонувати EWM як вбудований компонент S/4HANA (Embedded EWM). Це спрощує інтеграцію та знижує вартість ліцензування. Автономний (Decentralized) EWM підходить для мереж складів, які обслуговують кілька ERP-систем одночасно.

💡 Практичне правило: якщо у вас один основний склад з відносно стандартними процесами - Embedded EWM у S/4HANA є оптимальним. Якщо мережа розподілених складів із різними власниками або системами - розгляньте Decentralized EWM.

Кому потрібен EWM?

•       Рітейлери та e-commerce з великими розподільчими центрами

•       Виробники зі складними процесами пікінгу та комплектації

•       Логістичні оператори (3PL), що обслуговують кількох клієнтів

•       Компанії, що планують або вже впровадили складську автоматизацію

Перехід з WM на EWM - значний проект, що вимагає ретельного планування процесів. Але компанії, що зробили цей крок, як правило, фіксують зростання продуктивності складу на 20-40% та суттєве скорочення помилок при відвантаженні.
#SAP #SAPEWM #WarehouseManagement #Logistics #WMS #SAPЦікавинки

entry.title: Updated Stay Certified Programs for TM and EWM
entry.content:
We are glad to announce the updates of our Stay Certified programs for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Editon, Extended Warehouse Management and Transportation Management. You can find the new assets (recorded live sessions) via the following links:

  • Stay Certified for EWM 
  • Stay Certified for TM 

What is Stay Certified?

Stay Certified is SAP's program for maintaining up-to-date certifications by completing periodic update learning modules and assessments designed for specific roles.

Which certifications are associated with this update?

  • The Stay Certified program for EWM is associated with the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, Extended Warehouse Management certification (C_S4EWM).
  • The Stay Certified program for TM is associated with the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, Transportation Management certification (C_S4TM).

How do I take the Stay Certified assessment?

To take the Stay Certified assessment, follow these steps:

  1. Log in to the learning.sap.com account.
  2. Click on "My Certifications" under your profile. You will then see a list of all your certifications.
  3.  Click on "Manage certification". 

 

  1. Click on the "Take Assessment" button.

Note: Access to the Stay Certified programs requires a subscription on the learning.sap.com platform.

entry.title: SAP Business Client 8.10
entry.content:
Today we are happy to launch SAP Business Client 8.10, together with SAP GUI for Windows 8.10 .

This release is fully downward compatible with former releases. The following new features have been implemented:

 

Horizon Theming:

Morning Horizon Theme
SAP Business Client 8.10 offers Horizon theming, including Morning Horizon, Evening Horizon and HC-variants. Additionally, Custom Theming for Horizon themes is available for On-Premise and Cloud systems.

Joule : 

Together with SAP GUI for Windows 8.10, the SAP Business Client allows users to use Joule for SAP GUI for Windows transactions, enabling Joule to access the current transaction and apply Frontend Actions.

See more: Joule meets Classic UI

Edge Browser Extensions: 
With release 8.10, the SAP Business Client is open to Edge browser extensions, allowing companies to enhance their SAP Business Client experience.

Admin Command Line Toolset: 
This toolset allows an administrator to modify and change data and client behaviour via command line and possibly batch.

Index, New Tab and Service Map page in WPF: 
Formerly HTML-based internal pages are now recreated in WPF for easier theming and control.

Tab Navigation via JS:
Via an extra API in the WindowExternal-object, it's possible to create and manage IB-navigated tabs in the Fiori Launchpad via Javascript code.

Quicklaunch improvements:
The SAP Business Client 8.10 provides an improved Quicklaunch including autocomplete, fuzzy search, share, zoom and add favorite functionalities.

Enhanced Back and Forward Navigation UI: 
Back and Forward navigation buttons are available and allow access to the list of past navigation targets.

Restore Mechanism: 
Like in a standalone web browser, in case of an unlikely crash or simply after logon, it's possible to restore the tabs from the former session.

System Selection Screen Improvements: 
Including advanced filtering across workspaces and non-alphabetic ordering.

And Many More ...

 

For full usage of SAP GUI for Windows within SAP Business Client 8.10, SAP GUI for Windows 8.10 is required.

For details how to make use of all this new functionality, please see SAP Help Portal - SAP Business Client 8.10 .

Download

entry.title: Setting Up Expense Master Data in Commodity Expense Management
entry.content:
A Practical Guide for Business Users and Solution Designers

In Commodity Expense Management, setting up expense master data is not just a configuration activity—it’s a foundation for automation, accuracy, and financial transparency.

This blog walks through the structured approach to configuring expense master data, helping you understand how different applications work together to drive:

  • Intelligent expense determination
  • Automated accruals
  • Seamless financial postings

 

🧭 The Big Picture

For clarity and maintainability, expense master data is organized into four layers:

 

Basic

→             Define core expense objects

Foundation

→             Enable logic via fields & lookups

Mapping

→             Drive automation rules

Postings

→             Integrate with financial postings

Each layer builds on the previous one—so getting the design right upfront is critical.

 

1️⃣   Basic: Defining the Core Building Blocks

This is where you establish the what of expense management.

🔹Manage Expense Types

  • Logical grouping (e.g., Brokerage)
  • Enables business-semantic reporting
  • Supports multi-language descriptions
  • Where-used visibility

Lifecycle control:

  • An expense type can be de-activated to prevent any further usage if there are no active expense IDs.
  • An expense type can be deleted from the system if there are no expense IDs.

🔹Manage Expense IDs

Represents the actual expense.

Key design elements:

  • Mandatory link to Expense Type
  • Multi-language support
  • Where-used visibility

Lifecycle control:

  • An expense id can be de-activated to prevent any further usage if there are no active templates.
  • An expense id can be deleted from the system if there are no templates and transactional documents using it.

📌Firmed At vs Accrual Event

These two settings define when an expense becomes final and when it is financially recognized.

Firmed At

This setting defines when expense details (rate, service provider, payment terms, etc.) become final and unchangeable.

Options include:

  • Contract/Deal – Editable in Contract (Agri) / Deal (non-Agri), not beyond
  • Nomination – Editable up to Nomination stage
  • “-” – No restriction; editable across all documents

Accrual Event

This determines when an expense is considered incurred and should be accrued.

  • Contract Approved / Deal Published
  • Nomination Confirmed
  • Delivery Executed

👉Important Rule: Accrual Event ≥ Firmed At

🔹Manage Templates

Templates enable automation and defaulting of expenses during document creation (Contract or Deal).

A Template:

  • Contains a predefined list of expenses
  • Displays Expense Types, Firmed At, and Accrual Event indicators
  • Provides where-used visibility (via Lookups)

Lifecycle controls:

  • Deactivation (if not used in active lookups)
  • Deletion (if unused)

 

2️⃣   Foundation: Enabling Intelligent Logic

This layer defines the how behind determination.

🔹Manage Field Catalogs

Field Catalog define the input parameters used in Lookup Definitions.

Two field sources:

  • System – Predefined standard fields
  • User – Custom fields (namespace starting with Z)

Capabilities include:

  • Multi-language labels
  • Valid value definitions
  • Where-used tracking across Lookups
  • Fields can only be deleted if they are not referenced in any lookups

🔹Manage Look Up Definition

This is the most critical configuration step, as it governs how templates and expense details are determined. Two types of Lookups:

Template Determination

  • Identifies which Template to use
  • First field: Company Code (fixed)
  • Additional fields: configurable (System/User)

Expense Determination

  • Determines expense details (Service Provider, Rate, etc.)
  • First fields: Company Code and Expense ID (fixed)
  • Additional fields: configurable (System/User)

⚠️Once data is maintained → structure is locked

 

3️⃣  Mapping: Driving Automation

The Mapping layer operationalises the rules defined earlier to enable real-time determination.

🔹Manage Look Up Assignment

This step links Company Code → Lookup Definitions.

Key points:

  • One Template Lookup and one Expense Lookup per Company Code
  • Lookups can be reused across Company Codes
  • Missing assignment = no automation (manual entry of expenses required)

🔹Manage Template Determination

This app defines which Template gets selected based on input criteria.

Key characteristics:

  • Data maintained per Company Code
  • Dynamic structure based on Lookup Definition
  • Output field: Template ID
  • Allowed values: Values as defined in field Catalog and  “*” Any value

Now let us understand what this means with an example below

 

Commodity

Document Type

Incoterms

Template ID

SOYBEANS

3P Purchase

FOB

Template_SB_3P_FOB

SOYBEANS

3P Purchase

DDP

Template_SB_3P_DDP

SOYBEANS

3P Purchase

*

Template_SB_3P_ALL

So, when a contract (Agri) in this case is created the system looks out for exact match and if found then use the template for determining the list of expenses. If an exact match is not found, then the system looks out for a record by using generic search (from right to left) till it could determine a match to use the template for determining the list of expenses. If no match is found, then this results into no determination of expenses and therefore nothing is defaulted in contract.

🔹Manage Expense Determination

This defines how each Expense ID is populated with additional details such as:

  • Service Provider
  • Calculation Type
  • Rate / Amount
  • Currency, Units, UoM

Key capabilities:

  • Multi-provider support
  • Import/Export (Merge or Replace)
  • Upload logs with status tracking

Matching logic mirrors Template Determination:

  • Exact match → Use details
  • Generic match → Fallback logic
  • No match → Expense defaults without details

Below is a visual representation of how the top 3 layers stack up

 

4️⃣ Postings: Financial Integration

The Postings layer finalizes the setup by linking expenses to financial postings. This solution uses the Agency Business component for accruals and settlements.

🔹Manage Posting Assignments

Maps Expense ID to:

  • Pricing Condition Type
  • Accrual Document Type
  • Settlement Document Type
  • Offset Document Types

🔁Accrual & Settlement Behaviour

A key concept in Commodity Expense Management:

👉The system does NOT cancel postings
👉It creates offset entries

Accrual Posting (VBD)
                 ↓
Offset Accrual (Credit Memo)

Settlement Posting
                 ↓
Offset Settlement (Credit Memo)

⚠️Design Guideline:

  • Offset types must mirror original types
  • Must use Credit Memo category

 

🧠   Key Takeaways

  • Basic → Defines structure
  • Foundation → Enables logic
  • Mapping → Drives automation
  • Postings → Ensures financial accuracy

 

🚀   Final Thought

A well-designed expense master data setup enables:

✔️     Accurate planning

✔️     Intelligent automation

✔️     Real-time accruals

✔️     Clean financial postings

 

But more importantly—it creates a scalable and adaptable framework that can evolve with business complexity.

entry.title: SAP Commerce Cloud Q2 ‘26 Release Highlights
entry.content:
SAP Commerce Cloud Q2 2026 Release Highlights

The Q2 2026 release of SAP Commerce Cloud continues our commitment to delivering intelligent, composable, and scalable commerce experiences. This release introduces meaningful enhancements across storefront architecture, integrations, payments, and user experience, enabling organizations to simplify operations while accelerating time to value.

At the same time, unveiled a forward-looking set of AI-driven innovations and ecosystem partnerships that define the future direction of SAP Commerce Cloud at this year’s Sapphire. These capabilities are planned to be available later this year and provide a clear vision of how commerce will evolve toward more connected, intelligent, and autonomous experiences.

Together, the delivered innovations and upcoming capabilities demonstrate SAP’s continued investment in helping businesses innovate faster, adapt to change, and deliver exceptional customer journeys at scale.

AI-Driven Innovation and Ecosystem Expansion (SAPPHIRE Announcements)

SAP unveiled a new wave of AI-driven innovations and strategic partnerships at SAPPHIRE, offering a strong outlook on the future of SAP Commerce Cloud. These announcements outline SAP’s vision of seamlessly connecting discovery, conversion, and fulfillment through intelligent, AI-powered commerce experiences.

Highlights include AI Assistants for Commerce and expanded collaborations with Google and Vercel. These innovations are designed to embed intelligence across workflows, enable highly personalized customer interactions, and provide modern frontend flexibility. While not part of this release, they represent a significant step forward toward AI-first and ecosystem-driven commerce.

Decoupled Storefront Hosting Service

SAP Commerce Cloud introduces a new decoupled Storefront Hosting Service that separates frontend and backend builds, enabling faster deployment cycles and improved scalability. By isolating storefront delivery from core commerce operations, organizations gain greater flexibility in how they manage and evolve their digital experiences.

With capabilities such as server-side rendering, built-in caching, optional CDN integration, and significantly faster build times, merchants can deliver consistently high-performance storefronts. This results in improved customer experiences while reducing development bottlenecks and accelerating innovation cycles.

Composable AI Integration with MCP

Model Context Protocol (MCP) introduces a standardized approach for connecting AI agents and conversational interfaces with live commerce systems. This enables real-time interaction between AI tools and core commerce data, allowing assistants to provide accurate and up-to-date responses.

With MCP, conversational AI assistants can go beyond static responses by directly accessing live product catalogs, pricing, promotions, and inventory data, as well as executing transactional operations such as cart management and checkout. This transforms AI interactions into fully operational commerce experiences, unlocking new engagement 

With MCP, conversational AI assistants can go beyond static responses by directly accessing live product catalogs, pricing, promotions, and inventory data, as well as executing transactional operations such as cart management and checkout. This transforms AI interactions into fully operational commerce experiences, unlocking new engagement models and driving more intuitive customer journeys.

 

Service Provider Interfaces and Resilience

Service Provider Interfaces (SPI) extend SAP Commerce Cloud’s composability by enabling external services to plug into core commerce processes such as pricing, delivery, and tax calculation. This allows organizations to adopt best-in-class solutions without disrupting existing systems.

Combined with built-in resilience capabilities such as retries, circuit breakers, and timeout controls, SPI ensures stability in distributed architectures. Businesses benefit from both flexibility and reliability, allowing them to scale and innovate confidently in complex system landscapes.

OMS Flow Connector

The OMS Flow Connector simplifies integration across distributed order management environments by enabling seamless data flow between SAP Order Management Services, SAP solutions, and third-party systems. This eliminates the need for complex custom integrations and accelerates connectivity across the enterprise.

Business users can configure workflows and orchestrate integrations with minimal IT involvement, empowering teams to quickly adapt to changing business needs. This leads to faster process innovation, improved agility, and more efficient order orchestration across channels and fulfillment networks.

 

Open Payment Framework Enhancements

Enhancements to the Open Payment Framework introduce advanced split payment capabilities to support modern commerce use cases. Merchants can combine multiple payment methods within a single transaction, including gift cards and digital payments, while also supporting marketplace scenarios with multiple sellers.

These capabilities simplify complex payment orchestration and reduce friction at checkout. By enabling more flexible payment experiences, businesses can increase conversion rates while supporting evolving business models such as marketplaces and omnichannel commerce.

 

Wishlist Enhancements

Wishlist functionality has been improved to provide a more seamless customer experience across anonymous and authenticated journeys. Wishlists created before login can now be automatically merged with a user’s account, ensuring continuity of product interest and intent.

This enhancement reduces friction in the shopping journey and allows customers to effortlessly continue their experience across sessions and devices. As a result, businesses benefit from stronger engagement, better personalization opportunities, and improved conversion outcomes.

The Q2 2026 release reinforces SAP Commerce Cloud as a future-ready platform that combines performance, composability, and innovation. By delivering both immediate value and a strong vision for the future, SAP continues to help organizations build resilient, scalable, and differentiated commerce experiences.

 

 

For more details on the above release features, check out the following resources: 

  • SAP Commerce Cloud Release Navigator single access point to release content 
  • Replay of the Q2 2026 SAP Commerce Cloud Release Recap webcast 
  • Register for the Q2 2026 Tech-Focused Update webcast
  • Sign up for the monthly SAP Commerce newsletter
  • Read the SAP Customer Experience Q2 2026 blog

SAP Help Portal 

  • SAP Commerce Cloud What’s New 

2026 Holiday Season Readiness Program - Proactively Preparing for Peak with Confidence

  • Sign up for the Holiday Season Readiness Program

entry.title: Freight Order Quantity Analysis
entry.content:
In SAP Transportation Management in transportation execution and operational visibility scenarios. In one weekly review, the business team wanted to understand whether transportation volume was evenly distributed across carriers.

The first comparison was based mainly on the number of completed Freight Orders. At first glance, two carriers looked almost equal because their Freight Order counts were close. However, when we reviewed the transported gross weight and gross volume, we found that the actual workload was not the same.

For confidentiality, I have used representative carrier names and adjusted figures. The scenario is based on the type of operational review I have worked on, but the numbers are not production values.
Accessing the App the Freight Order Quantity Analysis app can be accessed from the SAP Fiori launchpad.

  1. Open the SAP Fiori launchpad.
  2. Search for Freight Order Quantity Analysis.
  3. Open the app with Fiori app ID F2676.
  4. From the overview page, select the relevant analytical card or data point.
  5. Navigate to the detailed view to analyze the Freight Orders behind the selected KPI.

The overview page provides different analytical cards, such as gross weight, gross volume, quantity distribution, carrier quantity analysis, shipper quantity analysis, ship to party quantity analysis, transportation mode and utilization.


Figure 1: Freight Order Quantity Analysis overview page - Fiori app F2676

Filters Used for the Analysis from the overview page, I selected the Carrier Quantity Analysis card and opened the detailed view. For the analysis, I used the following filter and display selections:

  • Transportation Mode Category: Road
  • Destination Country/Region: United States
  • View By: Carrier
  • Measure: Gross Weight
  • Display Weight Unit: Metric Tons
  • Display Volume Unit: Cubic Metres
  • Display Quantity Unit: Each
  • Completion Period: Selected completion weeks within the available current and previous month period

The standard app focuses on completed Freight Orders for the current and previous month. Because of this, I used the Completion Year/Week field to compare weekly movement trends.

These fields helped to compare the number of Freight Orders against the actual transportation volume.


Figure 2: Detailed view with carrier, transportation mode, units and completion week filters

Representative Carrier Comparison, The original business question was simple: Which carrier handled more work this week?
If we looked only at the number of Freight Orders, Carrier A appeared to have handled slightly more work than Carrier B.

Measure

Carrier A

Carrier B

Completed Freight Orders

42

39

Gross Weight

840 metric tons

1,260 metric tons

Gross Volume

1,960 m³

2,450 m³

Based on Freight Order count alone, Carrier A looked slightly higher because it completed 42 Freight Orders compared with Carrier B’s 39 Freight Orders. However, the quantity analysis showed a different story. Carrier B completed three fewer Freight Orders, but transported approximately 50% more gross weight. Carrier B also transported higher gross volume.
This finding changed the discussion. Carrier A had more documents, but Carrier B had a higher actual transportation workload. Carrier B was handling fewer but heavier movements, while Carrier A was handling a larger number of smaller shipments.

The main finding was that Freight Order count should not be used as the only measure of carrier workload. In this example, the business initially assumed that Carrier A had a slightly higher workload because it had more completed Freight Orders. After reviewing gross weight and gross volume, we could see that Carrier B had consumed more transportation capacity.
This was useful for three reasons:
1. It gave the team a better view of actual carrier workload.
2. It supported a more meaningful capacity discussion with carriers.
3. It helped explain why carrier utilization may look different from the document count.

The app also helped identify weekly movement patterns. When I changed the view to Completion Week, I could see the week in which Carrier B’s transported weight increased. This made it easier to review the underlying Freight Orders and understand what caused the change. Drilling Down into the Freight Orders after identifying the increase for Carrier B, I reviewed the detailed table and checked the Freight Orders behind the result.
Compared the following information:

  • Source and destination locations
  • Gross weight and volume
  • Number of containers
  • Transportation mode
  • Freight Order completion date
  • Carrier assignment

The list showed that Carrier B had handled several high weight movements to the same destination region during that week. This explained why the gross weight was higher even though the Freight Order count was lower. This is the part I found most useful. The overview helped identify the trend and the detailed view helped explain the reason behind it.


Figure 3: Carrier comparison showing Freight Order count, gross weight, and gross volume

It is also important to understand that this analysis is based on completed Freight Orders. Freight Orders that are still being planned or executed may not be represented in the same way. This can explain differences between the application and another operational report that includes planned or in execution documents.
From a technical perspective, the app uses the C_FrtOrdQuantityQuery CDS view. The C_FRTORDQUANTITYQUERY_CDS OData service must be activated in the SAP Gateway system. Users also need the required SAP Transportation Management and SAP Fiori authorizations to access the app and view the analytical data. No custom development was required for this analysis. The key requirements were correct Freight Order data, the required authorizations and activation of the supporting analytical service.

Conclusion
The Freight Order Quantity Analysis app gives better insight into transportation volumes and supports more informed decision making. It helps teams understand not just how many Freight Orders are moving, but also how much business volume is being executed. The app may look simple at first, but when used together with operational knowledge, it can provide useful insights that are not always visible from standard Freight Order reports.

Reference: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/e3dc5400c1cc41d1bc0ae0e7fd9aa5a2/612fbe25aed64068a7aca8e8e8339de1.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com

entry.title: Ensuring Quality in BADI Implementations with ABAP Unit
entry.content:
Introduction

In a previous article, I described how to identify and implement the BADI FIN_PROFIT_CENTER_VALIDATION to enforce business rules during Profit Center maintenance.

However, implementing a solution is only part of the job. Once the enhancement is in production, new requirements, bug fixes, and refactoring activities can easily introduce regressions if the validation logic is not properly tested.

This is where ABAP Unit becomes essential. By creating automated tests for enhancement implementations, we can verify business rules consistently and gain confidence when evolving the solution over time.

In this article, I will share a practical example of how I tested a BADI implementation using ABAP Unit based on a real-world validation scenario.

Why Testing a BAdI Matters

BADI implementations often contain critical business logic. In many projects, this logic evolves gradually as new requirements are added. Without automated tests, every change introduces a risk of unexpected side effects.

The validation implemented in FIN_PROFIT_CENTER_VALIDATION ensures that the Segment value remains consistent with the last digit of the Profit Center. Whenever this rule is violated, an error message must be generated and the maintenance process must be blocked.

The business rule is relatively simple:

Last DigitExpected Segment
0GERAL
1ATO
2NAO_ATO

Although the rule itself is straightforward, it is a perfect candidate for automated testing because it contains clearly defined inputs and expected outcomes.

Test Class Design

Before creating individual test cases, I structured the test class to maximize readability and reduce duplication. The objective was to make each test focus only on the business rule being verified.

The class uses standard setup and teardown methods to create and release the class under test. This guarantees that each test executes in isolation and avoids side effects from previous executions.

METHOD setup.
  CREATE OBJECT me->f_cut.
ENDMETHOD.

METHOD teardown.
  FREE me->f_cut.
  REFRESH me->messages.
ENDMETHOD.

The BADI method expects a FIN_ENH_PROFIT_CENTER structure as input and returns validation messages through the CT_MESSAGES parameter. Since several test cases share the same execution flow, I introduced a helper method named ASSERT_NO_VALIDATION_ERROR.

This helper method prepares test data, executes the BADI, handles unexpected runtime exceptions, and verifies that no validation message was raised.

METHOD assert_no_validation_error.
    " Given
    DATA(profit_center_data) = VALUE fin_enh_profit_center( validityenddate	               = `19000101`
                                                            controllingareaforedit         = `SI01`
                                                            profitcenterforedit	           = profitcenter
                                                            validityenddateforedit         = `99991231`
                                                            validitystartdate	             = `20260623`
                                                            profitcentername               = `Z999999999`
                                                            profitctrresponsiblepersonname = `DFARCIC`
                                                            profitcentercreationdate       = `20260623`
                                                            profitcentercreatedbyuser	     = `DFARCIC`
                                                            profitcenterstandardhierarchy	 = `0101`
                                                            segment                        = segment ).

    " When
    TRY.
        me->f_cut->if_badi_fin_pc_validation~execute( EXPORTING is_profit_center = profit_center_data
                                                      CHANGING  ct_messages      = me->messages ).
      CATCH cx_ble_runtime_error INTO DATA(lx_error).
        cl_abap_unit_assert=>fail( msg = lx_error->get_text( ) ).
    ENDTRY.

    " Then
    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_initial( act = me->messages ).
  ENDMETHOD.

Using a helper method significantly reduced code duplication and allowed the individual test methods to remain concise and focused on their specific scenario.

Implementing the Test Scenario

To validate the implementation comprehensively, I created tests covering three different categories of behavior.

The first category verifies situations where the last digit of the Profit Center is not one of the supported values (0, 1, or 2). In these cases, the validation should simply be ignored.

METHOD ignore_when_last_digit_not_012.
  ...
ENDMETHOD.

The second category focuses on invalid combinations. In the following example, the Profit Center ends with 0, which means the expected Segment should be GERAL. Since the supplied value is ATO, the validation must generate an error message.

METHOD error_when_last_digit_is_0.
    " Given
    DATA(profit_center_data) = VALUE fin_enh_profit_center( validityenddate	               = `19000101`
                                                            controllingareaforedit         = `SI01`
                                                            profitcenterforedit	           = `Z999999990`
                                                            validityenddateforedit         = `99991231`
                                                            validitystartdate	             = `20260623`
                                                            profitcentername               = `Z999999999`
                                                            profitctrresponsiblepersonname = `DFARCIC`
                                                            profitcentercreationdate       = `20260623`
                                                            profitcentercreatedbyuser	     = `DFARCIC`
                                                            profitcenterstandardhierarchy	 = `0101`
                                                            segment                        = `ATO` ).

    " When
    TRY.
        me->f_cut->if_badi_fin_pc_validation~execute( EXPORTING is_profit_center = profit_center_data
                                                      CHANGING  ct_messages      = me->messages ).
      CATCH cx_ble_runtime_error INTO DATA(lx_error).
        cl_abap_unit_assert=>fail( msg = lx_error->get_text( ) ).
    ENDTRY.

    " Then
    DATA(expected_messages) = VALUE fin_enh_pc_message_t( ( msgid = `ZFIN`
                                                            msgty = `E`
                                                            msgno = 001
                                                            msgv1 = `GERAL` ) ).

    cl_abap_unit_assert=>assert_equals( act = me->messages          " Data object with current value
                                        exp = expected_messages ).  " Data object with expected type
  ENDMETHOD.

Finally, positive test cases verify that valid combinations do not generate any validation messages.

METHOD validate_when_last_digit_is_1.

  me->assert_no_validation_error(
    profitcenter = 'Z999999991'
    segment      = 'ATO' ).

ENDMETHOD.
METHOD validate_when_last_digit_is_2.

  me->assert_no_validation_error(
    profitcenter = 'Z999999992'
    segment      = 'NAO_ATO' ).

ENDMETHOD.

Lessons Learned and Clean ABAP Considerations

One interesting aspect of this exercise is how testability influenced the overall design of the solution. The need to create clear and maintainable tests encouraged the use of small methods, descriptive names, and reusable helper methods.

The resulting test class follows several Clean ABAP principles:

  • Small and focused test methods
  • Meaningful and descriptive names
  • Helper methods to eliminate duplication
  • Explicit use of me-> for clarity
  • Minimal shared state between tests

Another takeaway is that even relatively small BADI implementations benefit from automated testing. The effort required to create the tests was modest, while the long-term benefits in maintainability and confidence are substantial.

Final Thoughts

Implementing a BAdI is only the first step. Ensuring that the implementation remains reliable over time is equally important.

ABAP Unit provided a simple and effective way to validate the business rules implemented in FIN_PROFIT_CENTER_VALIDATION while making future changes much safer.

If you are developing custom validations through enhancement spots or BAdIs, I strongly recommend investing a little extra time in automated tests—it pays off quickly.

👉I would be interested to hear how other teams are testing their BAdI implementations and validation frameworks.

entry.title: Implementing IFRS S1 and S2 Sustainability Disclosure Standards with End‑to‑End Reporting Flows
entry.content:
This article reflects the collective efforts of several contributors: Bilge Akdur, Elsa Sandoval Ruiz, Julia Sabrowski

Introduction

The IFRS® Sustainability Disclosure Standards, IFRS S1 (General Requirements for Disclosure of Sustainability-Related Financial Information) and IFRS S2 (Climate-Related Disclosures), issued in June 2023 and effective for annual reporting periods beginning on or after 1 January 2024, are a common baseline for sustainability-related financial disclosure. As a growing number of jurisdictions around the world are adopting these standards, organizations are increasingly expected to report on climate-related risks and opportunities comparable to those applied in financial reporting. Sustainability reporting is dynamic by design as it considers material impacts, risks, and opportunities. Investors, for example, can now look to sustainability KPIs as signals to move from mostly stewardship non-financial reporting to financial-grade sustainability analysis for sustainability strategic business decisions.

For organizations reporting under the European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) or implementing IFRS Sustainability Disclosure Standards, reporting requirements are increasingly interconnected across frameworks. Consequently, interoperability is considered a critical enabler for organizations to align sustainability data across multiple regulatory and voluntary standards. SAP Sustainability solutions adopt interoperability as a characteristic for companies reporting under multiple frameworks, which can increasingly consolidate their sustainability data management rather than duplicating effort across disconnected processes.

End-to-end sustainability reporting requires an auditable chain from operational cause through identification, collection, calculation, analysis, and disclosure. Point tools own a slice of that chain, while SAP owns the whole of it. SAP Sustainability Control Tower and SAP Sustainability Footprint Management together enable an end‑to‑end IFRS S1 and S2 sustainability reporting flow, including carbon footprint calculation, and climate- and sustainability-related data acquisition through to governed disclosures. SAP Sustainability Control Tower now provides SAP‑delivered IFRS S1 and S2 data points as master content, so organizations can manage IFRS S1 and S2 disclosures within the same governance framework they already use for ESRS reporting, while SAP Sustainability Footprint Management supplies the underlying emissions and activity data needed for IFRS S2 climate‑related metrics. This post walks through the integrated flow from initial setup and data integration through to AI‑assisted report creation.

Initial Setup and Master Data

The starting point for IFRS S1 and S2 reporting in SAP Sustainability Control Tower is the Manage Master Data application. Here, organizations configure the foundational structures that underpin their sustainability disclosures, including reporting structures such as legal entities, business locations, and organizational units, as well as the associated hierarchies that define the reporting boundary.

Now, the reporting requirements under IFRS S1 and S2, version 2023, are provided by SAP in the “Reporting Standard”, “Reporting Standard Version,” and “Data Points” dimensions in the Manage Master Data App as of July 1st, 2026.

For IFRS reporting specifically, the same organizational reporting structure used to support ESRS disclosures is reused. This means organizations already configured for CSRD reporting do not need to rebuild their hierarchy levels, fiscal year variants, and organizational mappings, providing a unified data foundation across both ESRS and IFRS disclosure requirements.

Metrics Coverage

In the Manage Metrics application, organizations can now access SAP-provided metrics mapped to IFRS S1 and S2 data points.

As of July 2026, SAP Sustainability Control Tower includes:

  • 24 quantitative metrics for IFRS S2 Climate-Related Disclosures, covering greenhouse gas emissions (Scope 1, 2, and 3) and carbon credits..
  • 57 qualitative metrics across IFRS S1 and S2, including:
    • IFRS S1 General Disclosures: 13 metrics
    • IFRS S1 and IFRS S2 shared General Disclosures: 15 metrics
    • IFRS S2 Climate-Related Disclosures: 29 metrics

To support interoperability, qualitative metrics that appear in both IFRS S1 and IFRS S2 are represented as a single metric where applicable. This avoids duplicating data entry for disclosures that serve both standards.

Data points that include explanatory content, implementation guidance, or reporting clarifications are out of scope and are not included in the delivered master data. Organizations can refer to the corresponding IFRS standards documentation for this information.

Within metric disclosure references, customers can see, when applicable, interoperability between disclosure requirements across standards such as ESRS and IFRS, helping organizations address multiple reporting frameworks through a single reporting effort.

Reporting Scope

Once metrics are available, organizations define their reporting scope: which material metrics apply to which parts of their reporting structure, and for which periods. The reporting scope configuration in SAP Sustainability Control Tower allows organizations to select IFRS-relevant metrics alongside their existing ESRS scope.

This is particularly relevant for organizations reporting under both ESRS and IFRS, where many climate-related disclosures overlap. Because SAP Sustainability Control Tower supports interoperability with standards in its metric definitions, organizations can configure their reporting scope once and satisfy data requirements for multiple frameworks without managing parallel reporting processes.

Data Acquisition

With the reporting scope defined, data acquisition follows established SAP Sustainability Control Tower patterns. Quantitative data for IFRS S2 climate metrics can be sourced through the same channels used for ESRS climate data:

  • SAP Sustainability Footprint Management integration for greenhouse gas emissions data across scope 1, 2 and 3, with configurable period granularity (monthly, quarterly, yearly) based on the active fiscal year variant.
  • SAP Sustainability Footprint Management energy and fuel mix metrics for IFRS‑relevant climate information, adding energy consumption, energy mix, fuel mix, energy content and sourcing type, calculated together with the emissions in a single run.
  • SAP Datasphere content packages for loading actuals from connected source systems.
  • File upload for data not available through system integration.
  • Inbound ODATA API for programmatic data ingestion from any source.

For qualitative metrics, the data collection process supports form-based collection with workflow governance. Organizations can group IFRS qualitative metrics within a single data collection form, assign processors and approvers, and manage the entire collection cycle through governed workflows. It also supports collection of the corporate summary while showing previously recorded data for comparison.

Emissions and Energy Data

For IFRS S2 greenhouse gas disclosures, SAP Sustainability Footprint Management calculates corporate emissions directly based on ERP master and transactional data, rather than from manual uploads or separate data silos. This creates one governed data foundation for Scope 1, 2 and 3 results that aligns climate metrics with the same operational events and structures used to run the business.

In the same calculation flow, the solution can also deliver energy‑related metrics such as energy consumption, energy mix, fuel mix, energy content and sourcing type, where these are relevant for IFRS S2 reporting. One run of the model, therefore, produces multiple climate‑related outputs: mandatory emissions data plus optional energy metrics to support transition analysis and target tracking.

Because activity data, emission factors and calculation logic are all traceable back to their source, the resulting IFRS S2 figures are easier to substantiate. When transferred into SAP Sustainability Control Tower via direct integration, this governance and lineage are preserved, connecting operational cause, calculation and disclosure in a single, auditable chain - eliminating the need for external transformation or aggregation steps.

Note: SAP Sustainability Control Tower's emissions-related Data Provider Interfaces (including SAP_SCT_DPI_Emission and SAP_SCT_DPI_Energy) provide the structure for ingesting this data from SAP and non-SAP sources.

Analytics

Analyze ESG Data application enables organizations to explore their IFRS-aligned sustainability performance. With the target-versus-actual visualization capabilities delivered in Q2 2026, organizations can compare their climate-related performance against defined targets directly within the application.

For IFRS S2, this is particularly relevant for transition plan monitoring: organizations can track progress toward their greenhouse gas reduction targets, visualize emissions trends across reporting periods, and drill down by reporting structure to identify areas requiring attention.

The flexible chart-specific configuration model allows independent control over metric selection, filters, drilldowns, and chart types. Users can display up to six charts in parallel and combine chart and table views for side-by-side analysis of different IFRS disclosure dimensions, such as comparing Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions performance across legal entities or time periods.

 

AI-Assisted Report Generation

Generate Sustainability Reports with AI is available free of charge to all SAP Sustainability Control Tower customers as a base AI feature. Today, customers can already use the Generic ESG Reporting Template to automatically draft ESG reports in multiple languages, preview and edit the content, and download the result in PDF or DOCX format, with dynamic AI‑driven revisions helping refine the final narrative.

Building on these capabilities, the Generate ESG Reports with AI application will include an IFRS‑specific report template as a roadmap item for H2 2026. This template will allow organizations to generate a structured IFRS sustainability disclosure report from the quantitative and qualitative data managed in SAP Sustainability Control Tower, drawing on the governed ESG data foundation to produce a draft aligned with IFRS S1 and S2 requirements. Using the IFRS template, AI‑assisted report generation will cover the core IFRS content pillars—Governance, Strategy, Risk Management, and Metrics and Targets—providing a standards‑aligned starting point that customers can further tailor to their specific context.

 Interoperability: ESRS and IFRS Side by Side

A key design principle behind the IFRS content in SAP Sustainability Control Tower is interoperability. Organizations reporting under both ESRS (for CSRD compliance) and IFRS (for jurisdictions adopting ISSB standards) can manage both from a single data foundation.

Where disclosure requirements overlap, particularly in general disclosures and climate-related reporting, SAP Sustainability Control Tower's metric definitions are designed so that data entered once can satisfy requirements across both frameworks. Within the metric general information, organizations can see the mapping between ESRS and IFRS disclosure requirements, reducing the effort required to maintain parallel reporting tracks.

Additional Information

Documentation on IFRS S1 and S2 metrics is available in SAP Help Portal for SAP Sustainability Control Tower.

Scenario Guidance for Corporate Carbon Calculation is available in SAP Help Portal for SAP Sustainability Footprint Management.

This article contains copyright material of the IFRS Foundation and SAP All rights reserved. Published by SAP under license from the IFRS Foundation. Reproduction and use rights are strictly limited.  For more information about the IFRS Foundation and rights to use its material please visit www.ifrs.org. © 2026 IFRS Foundation

entry.title: From pipelines to products- when your S/4HANA data becomes a product, not a pipeline.
entry.content:
What SAP actually changed: SAP becomes the producer

Data products are not new; the data-mesh community has argued for them since around 2019. What SAP did in Business Data Cloud (BDC) is structural: for SAP sources, SAP itself becomes the producer.

SAP-managed data products ship with the platform. They are pre-built extractions from S/4HANA and other SAP applications, landed and refreshed by SAP-managed pipelines into the BDC storage layer (HDLF), and published to the catalog with their business semantics attached. You do not build the extraction or its base semantics. You activate them, and build only the logic that is genuinely yours on top, which moves your scarce engineering effort from plumbing to differentiation. Where data-team bandwidth is the binding constraint, and for most organizations it is, that is the whole game.

This is not "only SAP data." There are three doors into the same governed catalog. Only the first is gated by an application purchase.

 

Why “zero-copy” is a fact, not a slogan

The foundation stores data products as open columnar files (Parquet, managed as Delta tables) in a managed object store built on SAP HANA Data Lake Files (HDLF). File-based is the key phrase. A warehouse locks data inside a proprietary format, so the only way to share it is to export a copy. An object store keeps data as open files in a bucket, and open files can be read by many engines at once without anyone copying anything.

This is the lakehouse principle applied inside SAP: one physical copy in open Delta format, read by SAP Datasphere, SAP Databricks, and SAP HANA Cloud through a shared catalog. Inside the BDC boundary, that means produce once and consume across all three engines without duplicating the data. One copy, many readers, that is the mechanism the market calls "zero-copy."

The full pipeline: how data actually lands in HDLF

For technical audiences, here is the honest end-to-end. CDS views are the extraction interface throughout, but the pipeline runs in two contexts. In the SAP-managed context, SAP owns standard, SAP-released CDS views built on the One Domain Model, its cross-application semantic model that keeps core entities consistent. In the customer-managed context, you create or identify the CDS entity yourself: a standard view, an extended or Z-CDS view, a custom business object, or a table. The same physical machinery moves both, and that machinery is what the rest of this section walks through.

 

 

 

Extraction at a glance, by source system

 

Cloud S/4HANA activates SAP-managed data products directly; everything else, on-prem S/4HANA, ECC, and non-SAP, enters through the customer-managed door. The source differs, the entry point does not: CDS views over the Cloud Connector for on-prem, extractors and tables via a Datasphere connection for ECC, database and file connections for third-party. Whatever the origin, one Replication Flow performs the universal physical write into HDLF as Delta Parquet, landing in a single governed catalog.

 

One product, end to end, and back

Follow a single product from source to intelligence and back. It leaves S/4HANA through SAP-managed CDS extraction and lands in the BDC foundation as governed Delta files. It appears in the catalog carrying its schema, semantics, and lineage: the "app store" moment where you browse before you install. The customer activates it, making it consumable.

From there the same single product serves everything. Datasphere models it; SAP Analytics Cloud plans and reports on it; and when a data-science team wants it, it is shared to SAP Databricks over Delta Sharing, appearing in Unity Catalog as a mounted table while the bytes stay in the foundation. What crosses the share is the table and its column-level meaning: names, types, keys, and comments, so the data scientist sees labeled, described columns rather than naked ones.
