What does not cross is the multidimensional model or the source-side access controls, measures, hierarchies, and data-access rules live in Datasphere and SAC, not in the shared file, and are re-established on the consumer side. Zero-copy is real at the boundary; meaning travels at the column level, not the model level.

When the loop closes, the data product is published back into BDC as a new governed data product through a federated remote share, live and zero-copy, not a physical import.

One honest boundary

Good advisory work separates what a platform will show from what it will guarantee. So, plainly: Delta Sharing transports tables, not multidimensional models. Measures, hierarchies, and data-access controls do not survive the protocol and must be re-established on the consuming side. The semantics that carry over are the structural ones (keys, column names, comments, data types), not the full analytic model. Say this up front and you will be trusted; let people discover it later and you will not.

Go Deeper On Delta Sharing: 

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/customer-managed-data-products-in-sap-bdc-what-really-happens-when-your/ba-p/14394675

The economics: what actually burns capacity

Most confusion about BDC cost comes from collapsing three things that are actually separate. Hold them apart and the economics stop being mysterious. This is the distinction solution architects and commercial teams have to be able to explain to a customer.

Illustrative split from a small worked example. Exact mix depends on data volume, refresh frequency, and workloads run.

The single most misunderstood point in the whole model: intelligent content does not consume capacity units. You license the application, but what burns capacity is the data products and infrastructure underneath it. Switching on more applications barely moves the meter. Cost follows the workloads you actually run on your data (provisioning, refresh, queries, and any optional ML in Databricks), not the number of applications you own.

Why this matters now

The reason the industry is talking about governed data products in 2026 is not nostalgia for data-mesh theory. It is AI. Agents and copilots fail in production mostly because they lack business context: they do not know that this table means settlement, or that this field is a posted invoice. A governed data product is that context, delivered with the data. The same foundation that ends your dashboard disagreements is what makes your S/4HANA data trustworthy enough to hand to an AI.

So the shift from pipelines to products is really two shifts at once. It retires a decade of brittle, unowned extractions and their hidden maintenance cost. And it builds the governed, meaningful foundation the next wave of enterprise AI actually requires, data that is AI-ready because it is governed and meaningful, not merely moved faster.

That is what changes when your S/4HANA data becomes a product, not a pipeline.

SAP Enterprise Architecture Designer SAP Datasphere SAP Databricks SAP Analytics Cloud, connectivity SAP Business Data Cloud

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Safekeeper 2022 Is Here: What It Means for Your Upgrade Journey
entry.content:
Announcing the Release of SAP S/4HANA Safekeeper 2022

As outlined in SAP Note 3424346, SAP has released SAP S/4HANA Safekeeper 2022.

Although this is not a new SAP product, it represents an important milestone for customers running SAP S/4HANA 2022. Safekeeper is released for specific SAP S/4HANA innovation releases to help eligible customers continue operating their existing release after the end of mainstream maintenance, while they plan and execute an upgrade to a newer release under mainstream maintenance.

The availability of SAP S/4HANA Safekeeper 2022 gives customers additional flexibility to remain on SAP S/4HANA 2022 for a limited period while preparing their transition to a more recent SAP S/4HANA release.

Why is there a three-year gap before the next Safekeeper release?

One question customers may notice is why the next planned Safekeeper release—for SAP S/4HANA 2023—is expected only three years later.

The answer lies in SAP's updated SAP S/4HANA release strategy, introduced in 2022.

Beginning with SAP S/4HANA 2023, SAP implemented several significant lifecycle improvements:

  • A two-year innovation release cycle, replacing the previous annual cadence.

  • Seven years of mainstream maintenance for each innovation release, extended from the previous five-year period.

  • Feature Package Stacks (FPS) that deliver continuous innovation in smaller, easier-to-adopt increments without requiring customers to move immediately to the next innovation release.

These changes provide customers with longer planning horizons, greater operational stability, and more predictable upgrade cycles. Consequently, lifecycle milestones—including future Safekeeper releases—are now naturally spaced further apart.

What does this mean for SAP S/4HANA 2022 customers?

For organizations currently running SAP S/4HANA 2022, the release of SAP S/4HANA Safekeeper 2022 provides additional flexibility when planning their application lifecycle.

Customers who need more time beyond mainstream maintenance to complete testing, business validation, or transformation initiatives can leverage Safekeeper (subject to the applicable commercial terms and eligibility criteria) while preparing for their upgrade.

However, Safekeeper should be viewed as a bridge—not a destination. Customers are encouraged to define their upgrade roadmap early so they can benefit from the innovations, security enhancements, and longer maintenance horizons available in newer SAP S/4HANA releases.

Why SAP S/4HANA 2023 and 2025 deserve serious consideration

Customers upgrading from SAP S/4HANA 2022 will almost certainly target either SAP S/4HANA 2023 or SAP S/4HANA 2025.

These releases deliver far more than a longer maintenance timeline.

SAP S/4HANA 2023: The beginning of SAP's next-generation ERP lifecycle

SAP S/4HANA 2023 is the first release built on SAP's new lifecycle strategy, providing seven years of mainstream maintenance and a more predictable two-year innovation cadence.

It also marks the beginning of the Business AI era for SAP S/4HANA. For eligible SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition deployments, SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS01 introduces support for Joule, SAP's generative AI copilot. Users can interact with business applications using natural language, receive contextual assistance, and streamline navigation across business processes.

Combined with continuous innovation delivered through Feature Package Stacks, SAP S/4HANA 2023 establishes a strong long-term platform for customers modernizing their ERP landscape.

SAP S/4HANA 2025: Building on the foundation

SAP S/4HANA 2025 builds on the 2023 foundation with broader Business AI capabilities and further enhancements across the application.

Highlights include:

  • Expanded Joule integration and additional AI-powered business scenarios.

  • Enhanced Fiori user experience with refined applications and embedded insights.

  • Stronger Clean Core support through additional released APIs, business events, and extensibility options.

  • Functional enhancements across Finance, Supply Chain, Manufacturing, Asset Management, Sustainability, and other business areas.

  • A mainstream maintenance horizon extending to approximately 2032, providing customers with an even longer period of lifecycle stability.

For organizations beginning a new implementation or planning a major upgrade over the next few years, SAP S/4HANA 2025 is likely to be the preferred target release, balancing innovation, stability, and long-term support.

Final thoughts

The release of SAP S/4HANA Safekeeper 2022 provides customers with valuable flexibility, allowing additional time to prepare for their next SAP S/4HANA upgrade where needed.

At the same time, it reinforces the importance of proactive lifecycle planning. Whether the destination is SAP S/4HANA 2023 or SAP S/4HANA 2025, customers have an opportunity to move beyond simply remaining supported and instead take advantage of SAP's latest innovations in Business AI, Clean Core, user experience, and continuous functional enhancements.

Safekeeper helps extend the runway—but the real value lies in reaching the next generation of SAP S/4HANA.

entry.title: 【DMLTデータマネジメントブログ】Vol.1.1 - M&A とデータマネジメント なぜ今、企業M&AにおけるSAP ERP対応が重要課題となるのか
entry.content:
はじめに

DMLTデータマネジメントブログ連載、今回はVol.1 - M&Aとデータマネジメントの1回目をお届けします。

Vol.1：M&A とデータマネジメント
　　　1.1：なぜ今、企業M&AにおけるSAP ERP対応が重要課題となるのか
　　　1.2：SAPシステム分離はなぜ難しいのか
　　　1.3：DMLTは企業M&AにおけるSAP ERP対応をどのように支援するのか

Vol.2：Autonomous Enterprise に備えるマスターデータ品質（運用）
Vol.3：その“データ”、活かすための準備はできていますか？「Data Management Maturity Assessment」とは 
Vol.4：Data Health Check と Data Volume Management
Vol.5：Information Lifecycle Management（ILM）
Vol.6：Data Privacy (1) — GDPR と PII への対応
Vol.7：Data Privacy (2) — Masking Solution
Vol.8：大容量システム移行やダウンタイム短縮とデータマネジメント
Vol.9：法規制やビジネス変化に伴う、基幹システムの会計領域を中心としたトランスフォーメーション

Vol1.1：なぜ今、企業M&AにおけるSAP ERP対応が重要課題となるのか

近年、日本企業を取り巻く経営環境は大きく変化しています。
グローバル競争の激化、事業ポートフォリオ見直し、DX推進、選択と集中といった経営戦略のもと、事業売却やグループ再編などの企業M&Aが活発化しています。

その中で、近年特に重要テーマとなっているのが、SAP ERPをはじめとする基幹システムへの対応です。
M&Aでは、法務・財務・人事など多くの論点が存在しますが、実際には「Day1から事業を継続できるか」という観点で、ERP対応が最重要課題となるケースも少なくありません。

本記事では、なぜ企業M&AにおいてSAP ERP対応が重要になるのか、その背景と難しさについて整理します。

ERPは企業活動そのものを支える基盤

SAP ERPは単なる業務システムではありません。会計、販売、購買、在庫、生産、原価管理など、企業活動を横断的に支えるミッションクリティカルな基盤として、多くの企業で長年利用されています。
そのため、企業M&AにおいてSAP ERP対応が必要になる場合、単純なデータコピーやシステム移行では済まないケースが多く発生します。

例えば、事業売却後のDay1においても、

  • 顧客への出荷
  • 請求処理
  • サプライヤへの発注
  • 在庫管理
  • 月次決算
  • 原価計算

などの業務を停止することはできません。M&A成立日に合わせて、新たな組織・新たなシステム環境で業務を継続させる必要があります。つまり、ERP対応とは単なるIT移行ではなく、「事業継続そのもの」を支える活動とも言えます。

なぜSAP ERPのM&A対応は難しいのか

特にSAP ERPは、多くの企業で10年以上にわたり利用され、業務プロセスや周辺システムと深く結びついています。そのため、M&A時のシステム分離・統合では、単純な会社コード単位の切り分けだけでは対応できないケースが多く存在します。

特に事業売却（Carve-out）では、

  • 一部事業のみ対象
  • 一部製品のみ対象
  • 一部顧客のみ対象
  • 一部工場のみ対象

といった形で、会社組織よりさらに細かい単位でのデータ整理や移行が必要になることがあります。また、ERP内部では会計データだけでなく、販売・購買・在庫・生産・原価などのデータが相互に関連しています。そのため、一部データだけを単純に切り出すと、

  • 会計整合性が崩れる
  • 在庫数量が合わない
  • 未決済データが不整合になる
  • 原価計算に影響する

といった問題につながる可能性があります。

さらに、多くの企業ではSAP ERP以外にも、周辺システムや外部インターフェースが多数存在します。ERP分離・統合では、こうしたシステム間連携も含めて整理・再設計する必要があります。

M&A案件特有の難しさ

「短納期」「秘匿性」「延期できない」
M&A案件には、通常のERP導入プロジェクトとは異なる特徴があります。その一つが、極めて高い秘匿性です。

M&A案件は経営上の重要機密であり、限られた関係者のみで検討が進められるケースが一般的です。そのため、IT部門やシステム担当者が本格的に参画できるタイミングが限られることも少なくありません。一方で、M&A成立後のDay1には確実に業務を継続する必要があります。

つまり、

  • 限られた期間
  • 限られたメンバー
  • 限られた情報

の中で、

  • システム分離方式
  • データ移行範囲
  • 業務継続方法
  • Cutover計画

などを短期間で整理しなければなりません。さらに、契約締結日や法的要件に紐づくため、通常のシステムプロジェクトのように簡単に延期できないという特徴もあります。

TSA（Transition Service Agreement）という現実的な選択肢

M&A案件では、Day1時点ですべてのシステム分離を完了できないケースも少なくありません。その場合に利用されるのが、TSA（Transition Service Agreement）です。

TSAとは、売却後も一定期間、売り手企業のシステムや業務サービスを継続利用するための契約形態です。

例えば、

  • 新会社システム構築に時間が必要
  • 周辺システム分離に時間がかかる
  • グローバルシステムとの切り離しが難しい

といった場合、一時的に既存SAP ERPを継続利用するケースがあります。

しかし、TSA期間中も、

  • データアクセス制御
  • 権限管理
  • 業務責任範囲
  • 最終分離計画

などを適切に整理する必要があります。つまり、M&A対応では単純な「移行」ではなく、段階的な分離と業務継続を前提としたアプローチが求められます。

早期アセスメントの重要性

M&AにおけるSAP ERP対応では、技術だけでなく、業務継続や移行戦略を含めた総合的な検討が必要になります。

特に、初めてM&Aを経験する企業では、

  • 何から検討すべきか分からない
  • どこにリスクがあるか把握できない
  • スケジュール感が見えない

という課題が発生することもあります。

そのため、早い段階からERP観点でのアセスメントを実施し、

  • 分離対象
  • データ範囲
  • 業務継続方法
  • TSA要否
  • システム影響

などを整理することが重要になります。

おわりに

企業M&Aにおいて、SAP ERP対応は単なるデータ移行ではありません。それは、M&A成立後も事業を継続するための重要な基盤整備であり、短期間かつ高い制約条件の中で実施する必要があります。特に事業売却を伴うCarve-outでは、複雑なデータ分離や業務継続設計が求められます。

次回は、「SAPシステム分離はなぜ難しいのか」をテーマに、SAP ERP特有の複雑性や、ERP分離で検討すべきポイントについて、さらに詳しく解説します。

お問い合わせ

M&Aに対してSAPでどのような対応が可能なのか、どのように備えることができるのか、についてのお問い合わせはこちらsap_dmlt_gce@sap.com

次回：Vol.1.2 – SAPシステム分離はなぜ難しいのか

entry.title: 【DMLTデータマネジメントブログ】Vol.1.2 - M&A とデータマネジメント SAPシステム分離はなぜ難しいのか
entry.content:
DMLTデータマネジメントブログ連載、今回はVol.1 - M&Aとデータマネジメントの2回目をお届けします。

Vol.1：M&A とデータマネジメント

　　　1.2：SAPシステム分離はなぜ難しいのか
　　　1.3：DMLTは企業M&AにおけるSAP ERP対応をどのように支援するのか

Vol.2：Autonomous Enterprise に備えるマスターデータ品質（運用）
Vol.3：その“データ”、活かすための準備はできていますか？「Data Management Maturity Assessment」とは 
Vol.4：Data Health Check と Data Volume Management
Vol.5：Information Lifecycle Management（ILM）
Vol.6：Data Privacy (1) — GDPR と PII への対応
Vol.7：Data Privacy (2) — Masking Solution
Vol.8：大容量システム移行やダウンタイム短縮とデータマネジメント
Vol.9：法規制やビジネス変化に伴う、基幹システムの会計領域を中心としたトランスフォーメーション

Vol1.2：SAPシステム分離はなぜ難しいのか

企業M&AにおけるERP分離の複雑性とは

前回の記事では、近年の企業M&A増加の背景と、その中でSAP ERP対応が重要課題となる理由について紹介しました。
特に、事業売却やグループ再編においては、M&A成立後のDay1から業務を継続させる必要があり、ERP対応が単なるIT移行ではなく、事業継続そのものを支える活動になることを説明しました。

今回は、その中でも特に難易度が高いと言われる「SAPシステム分離」の複雑性について解説します。

SAP ERPは“企業活動そのもの”を保持している

SAP ERPは、単一の業務システムではありません。
会計、販売、購買、在庫、生産、原価管理など、企業活動を横断的に支える統合基幹システムとして、多くの企業で長期間運用されています。そのため、M&A時に「一部事業だけを切り出したい」と考えても、単純にデータをコピーすれば済むわけではありません。

例えば、ある製品事業を売却するケースを考えてみます。

一見すると、
「対象事業に関係するマスターデータと伝票データを抽出すれば良い」
ように見えるかもしれません。

しかし実際には、

  • 販売データ
  • 購買データ
  • 在庫データ
  • 生産データ
  • 原価データ
  • 会計データ

などが相互に関連しており、一部データだけを切り出すと整合性が崩れる可能性があります。

SAP ERPにおけるシステム分離とは、単なるデータ抽出ではなく、企業活動全体を矛盾なく再構成する作業とも言えます。

「会社コード単位で分ければ良い」とは限らない

SAP ERPにおけるM&A対応で、特に難易度が高いのが事業売却（Carve-out）です。
会社売却であれば、会社コード単位で比較的整理しやすいケースもあります。

しかし、実際のM&Aでは、

  • 特定事業のみ売却
  • 一部製品ラインのみ売却
  • 特定顧客向け事業のみ分離
  • 一部工場のみ対象

といったケースが多く存在します。

つまり、SAP上の組織構造と、実際の売却対象が一致しないことが少なくありません。
さらに、同一会社コード内で複数事業が混在しているケースでは、

  • どの伝票を移行対象とするのか
  • どの在庫を切り分けるのか
  • 共通利用しているマスタをどう扱うのか

といった整理が必要になります。

また、長年利用されているSAP ERPでは、過去の組織変更や運用変更により、データ構造がさらに複雑化していることもあります。そのため、M&A対応では、単純な組織単位ではなく、業務実態に即したデータ分析と整理が重要になります。

会計だけでは成立しないERP分離

ERP分離を検討する際、まず会計データに注目するケースは多くあります。しかし、実際には会計データだけを移行しても、業務継続は成立しません。

例えば、

  • 未出荷の受注
  • 未入荷の発注
  • 移動中在庫
  • 生産途中の製造指図
  • 未請求データ
  • 未決済債権債務

など、Day1時点で継続中の業務データをどう扱うかが非常に重要になります。また、在庫データについても、単純に数量を移行するだけでは十分ではありません。評価額、原価、移動平均単価、ロット管理など、会計や物流に影響する情報との整合性を維持する必要があります。

さらに、生産管理を利用している企業では、

  • BOM
  • 作業手順
  • 生産計画
  • 原価積算

なども含めた検討が必要になります。

つまり、ERP分離では「どのモジュールを使っているか」だけではなく、「実際の業務がどのようにつながっているか」を理解したうえで、移行方針を設計する必要があります。

業務継続を前提とした分離設計

M&A案件では、Day1から業務を止められないという制約があります。そのため、SAPシステム分離では、単純な移行だけでなく、業務継続を前提とした設計が求められます。

例えば、

  • どのタイミングでシステムを切り替えるのか
  • 移行期間中の業務をどう継続するのか
  • データ凍結期間をどこまで許容できるのか
  • 周辺システムとの接続をどう維持するのか

など、多くの論点を短期間で整理する必要があります。また、すべてをDay1で完全分離できない場合には、TSA（Transition Service Agreement）を前提とした段階的な分離も現実的な選択肢となります。

その場合でも、

  • 権限制御
  • データ参照範囲
  • 業務責任分界
  • 最終分離計画

などを明確に整理しなければなりません。つまり、SAP ERP分離では、「移行そのもの」だけではなく、「移行後にどう業務を成立させるか」まで含めた設計が重要になります。

なぜ早期アセスメントが重要なのか

M&A案件では、短期間での意思決定が求められます。
一方で、SAP ERPの分離・統合は、企業ごとに業務構造やデータ構造が大きく異なります。
そのため、過去の経験だけで単純に難易度を判断することはできません。

例えば、

  • どの事業範囲を対象とするのか
  • どこまで過去データを保持するのか
  • 周辺システム影響はどこまであるのか
  • TSAが必要か
  • Day1までに何を優先すべきか

などを、初期段階で整理する必要があります。
特に、M&A案件ではIT部門の検討開始が遅れるケースも多く、限られた時間で現実的な移行アプローチを定義することが重要になります。

おわりに

SAP ERPのシステム分離は、単なるデータ移行ではありません。
それは、複雑に連携した企業活動を維持しながら、新たな事業構造へ再構成する取り組みです。

特にM&AにおけるCarve-outでは、

  • 複雑なデータ依存関係
  • 業務継続要件
  • 短納期
  • TSA対応

など、多くの制約条件を踏まえた検討が必要になります。

次回は、「DMLTによる企業M&A対応アプローチ」をテーマに、SAP DMLTがどのようにM&A案件におけるERP分離・統合を支援しているのか、具体的な考え方やアプローチについて紹介します。

お問い合わせ

M&Aに対してSAPでどのように対応が可能なのか、どのように備えることができるのか、についてのお問い合わせはこちらsap_dmlt_gce@sap.com

次回：Vol.1.3 – DMLTによる企業M＆A対応アプローチ

entry.title: 【DMLTデータマネジメントブログ】Vol.1.3 - M&A とデータマネジメント DMLTは企業M&AにおけるSAP ERP対応をどのように支援するのか
entry.content:
はじめに

DMLTデータマネジメントブログ連載、今回はVol.1 - M&Aとデータマネジメントの最終回をお届けします。

Vol.1：M&A とデータマネジメント
　　　1.1：なぜ今、企業M&AにおけるSAP ERP対応が重要課題となるのか
　　　1.2：SAPシステム分離はなぜ難しいのか
　　　1.3：DMLTは企業M&AにおけるSAP ERP対応をどのように支援するのか

Vol.2：Autonomous Enterprise に備えるマスターデータ品質（運用）
Vol.3：その“データ”、活かすための準備はできていますか？「Data Management Maturity Assessment」とは 
Vol.4：Data Health Check と Data Volume Management
Vol.5：Information Lifecycle Management（ILM）
Vol.6：Data Privacy (1) — GDPR と PII への対応
Vol.7：Data Privacy (2) — Masking Solution
Vol.8：大容量システム移行やダウンタイム短縮とデータマネジメント
Vol.9：法規制やビジネス変化に伴う、基幹システムの会計領域を中心としたトランスフォーメーション

Vol1.3：DMLTは企業M&AにおけるSAP ERP対応をどのように支援するのか

業務継続を支えるデータ変革アプローチ

これまでの記事では、近年の企業M&A増加の背景と、SAP ERPにおけるシステム分離・統合の難しさについて解説しました。
特に事業売却（Carve-out）では、

  • 単純な会社コード単位では分離できない
  • 会計・物流・在庫・原価などが密接に連携している
  • Day1から業務を継続する必要がある
  • 短納期かつ延期が難しい

といった特徴があり、通常のERP導入プロジェクトとは異なる難しさが存在します。
今回は、こうしたM&A案件に対して、SAP DMLT（Data Management and Landscape Transformation）がどのようなアプローチで支援しているのかをご紹介します。

DMLTは単なる「データ移行チーム」ではない

DMLTというと、「SAPのデータ移行サービス」というイメージを持たれることがあります。
しかし、企業M&AにおけるSAP ERP対応では、単純なデータ移行だけでは解決できない課題が多く存在します。

例えば、

  • どのデータを分離対象とするのか
  • Day1までに何を優先すべきか
  • TSAを前提にどのような段階移行を行うのか
  • 過去データをどこまで保持するのか
  • 業務継続をどう成立させるのか

など、技術だけでなく、業務や運用を含めた整理が必要になります。そのため、DMLTでは単純な移行作業ではなく、「企業活動を継続させるためのデータ変革」を重視したアプローチを行っています。

まず重要になるのは「初期アセスメント」

M&A案件では、検討開始からDay1までの期間が限られているケースが少なくありません。また、経営判断や契約条件との関係から、IT側で自由にスケジュールを変更できないことも多くあります。そのため、早期段階で以下を整理することが重要になります。

  • 分離対象となる事業範囲
  • SAP ERP上の組織構造
  • 業務継続要件
  • 周辺システム影響
  • データ保持要件
  • TSA要否
  • Day1で必要となる機能

例えば、「会社売却」なのか、「一部事業売却」なのかによって、分離難易度は大きく変わります。
また、同じCarve-out案件でも、

  • 製品単位
  • 顧客単位
  • 地域単位
  • 工場単位

など、どの観点で事業を切り分ける必要があるかによって、必要なデータ整理や移行方式は大きく異なります。
DMLTでは、SAP ERP内部のデータ構造だけではなく、実際の業務運用やM&A後のオペレーションも踏まえながら、現実的な移行アプローチを整理していきます。

「業務継続」を前提とした移行設計

M&A案件では、「システムを移行できるか」ではなく、「業務を止めずに移行できるか」が重要になります。

特にDay1では、

  • 出荷
  • 請求
  • 購買
  • 在庫管理
  • 生産
  • 月次決算

などの業務を継続させる必要があります。
そのため、DMLTでは単純なデータ移行だけではなく、業務継続を前提とした移行設計を重視しています。

例えば、

  • どのデータをDay1で移行するか
  • どのデータを後続移行とするか
  • 未決済データをどう扱うか
  • 移行中の業務停止時間をどう最小化するか

などを、業務影響を踏まえて整理します。また、すべてを一度に分離することが難しい場合には、TSA（Transition Service Agreement）を前提とした段階的な分離アプローチを検討するケースもあります。

その場合でも、

  • データアクセス制御
  • 権限管理
  • システム責任分界
  • 最終分離タイミング

などを含めて整理する必要があります。
つまり、M&AにおけるSAP ERP対応では、「移行作業」そのものよりも、「移行後にどう業務を成立させるか」が重要になります。

SAP ERP全体を横断した検討が必要

SAP ERPでは、会計だけを切り離して対応できるケースはほとんどありません。

例えば、

  • 在庫数量と会計残高
  • 原価計算と生産実績
  • 購買と支払
  • 販売と請求

など、各モジュールが密接に関連しています。そのため、一部データのみを単純に切り出すと、業務整合性が崩れる可能性があります。特に長年利用されているSAP ERPでは、

  • 過去組織変更
  • カスタマイズ
  • 周辺システム連携
  • 独自運用

などが積み重なっているケースも多く、システム構造の把握自体が難しいこともあります。

DMLTでは、SAP ERP全体を横断的に分析しながら、

  • どのデータを保持するのか
  • どのデータを分離するのか
  • どのデータを再構成する必要があるのか

を整理し、整合性を維持した移行を支援します。

M&A対応では「経験知」が重要になる

M&A案件では、顧客自身が初めてERP分離・統合を経験するケースも少なくありません。

その場合、

  • どこにリスクがあるのか
  • 何を優先すべきか
  • どこまでDay1で対応すべきか

といった判断が難しくなることがあります。
また、M&A案件は秘匿性が高く、限られた期間・限られたメンバーで進行するケースも多いため、短期間で現実的なアプローチを整理する必要があります。

そのため、SAP ERPの技術知識だけでなく、

  • 業務継続
  • Cutover
  • TSA
  • 段階移行
  • データ整合性

などを含めた経験知が重要になります。
DMLTでは、こうしたM&A特有の制約条件を踏まえながら、顧客状況に応じたアプローチ検討を支援しています。

おわりに

企業M&AにおけるSAP ERP対応は、単なるデータ移行プロジェクトではありません。
それは、複雑に連携した企業活動を維持しながら、新たな事業構造へ移行するための取り組みです。

特にCarve-outでは、

  • 複雑なデータ分離
  • 業務継続
  • TSA対応
  • 短納期
  • Day1制約

など、多くの課題を同時に整理する必要があります。
DMLTでは、SAP ERPに関するデータと業務の両面から、M&A案件における現実的な移行アプローチを支援しています。
今後、企業M&Aや事業再編がさらに加速していく中で、ERP観点での早期検討はますます重要になっていくと考えられます。

お問い合わせ

M&Aに対してSAPでどのように対応が可能なのか、どのように備えることができるのか、についてのお問い合わせはこちらsap_dmlt_gce@sap.com

次回：Vol.2 - Autonomous Enterprise に備えるマスターデータ品質（運用）

entry.title: Trapped in Transformation OR The Uncomfortable Truth of the EA in an S/4HANA Transformation
entry.content:
AI generated

 

Imagine this: a medium-sized fashion company—agile, successful, but with an IT landscape that has grown organically over the years… or, put differently: wildly.

The heart of its process’s beats in three different rhythms—three metronomes, none synchronized: Fxxxxx for retail, Ixxxxx for wholesale, and Nxxxxxxx for financial accounting & controlling. This kind of fragmentation is expensive, inefficient, and a serious brake on digital transformation. The logical consequence: a comprehensive transformation to SAP S/4HANA for fashion and retail.

A mammoth project. And right in the middle of it: the Enterprise Architect (EA).

The textbook role: the EA as a strategic enabler

The classic picture of the Enterprise Architect revolves around technology: systems, platforms, integrations, interfaces. They think in terms of architectural layers, evaluate tools, and safeguard the technical landscape. That matters—but it isn’t enough.

An EA who truly makes an impact doesn’t think in systems. They think in the business. They understand how processes work, where decisions are made, what the strategic goals are—and which technological choices steer the company in a certain direction. Every architecture decision is therefore also a business decision: it defines what becomes possible and what doesn’t, what costs and what saves, what scales and what slows things down.

An EA who speaks only in IT categories will be tolerated. An EA who thinks like a business leader and actively steers through decisions will be heard—and needed.

Transformation as a permanent state

In the past, transformation was a clearly defined project with a beginning, a middle, and an end. Today, it’s a continuous operating mode—and it devours resources. The longer a transformation runs, the more internal employees get pulled into roles that aren’t really theirs. The project manager becomes the test coordinator, the executive assistant becomes the change manager, the architect becomes the data migration lead.

This kind of role drift isn’t the exception—it’s the pattern. It’s reinforced by a company spirit that not only tolerates taking on new roles but actively encourages it. Anyone willing to step outside their comfort zone isn’t punished in a transformation—they’re needed.

Companies systematically underestimate how much internal know-how a transformation consumes—and how many people inevitably have to outgrow themselves along the way. That creates pressure, but it also creates an opportunity: if you perform well in an unfamiliar role, you come back stronger. You grow beyond yourself—usually when you have no other choice.

And the EA? In this environment, they must guarantee stability and enable change—while they themselves have long since ended up in a role that was never part of the plan.

Trapped between aspiration and reality

The classic EA aspiration

The traditional image of the Enterprise Architect is that of a strategic designer: they design target architectures, define standards, create transparency across the enterprise landscape, and derive recommendations for action. Frameworks like TOGAF provide structured approaches for this.

The transformative reality

In practice, however, the EA often faces a very different reality:

  • Target architectures become outdated before they’re fully implemented
  • Decisions are made decentralized—without EA involvement
  • Technical debt grows faster than it can be reduced
  • Stakeholders decide by time and budget—while architectural sustainability politely waits outside the meeting invite.
  • Enterprise architecture documentation lags reality

The EA ends up trapped between what should be—and what actually is.

What this means for the EA role

1) From planner to enabler

The EA can no longer act primarily as a central planner. Instead, they must become an enabler and coach—someone who empowers decentralized teams to make architecturally sound decisions without having to personally make every decision.

“Architecture is not about control; it's about enabling good decisions at scale.”

2) From static models to a living landscape

Instead of complex, one-off architecture models, what’s needed are lightweight artifacts that are maintained continuously: Architecture Decision Records (ADRs), living wikis, automatically generated landscape maps. The model has to be able to breathe.

3) From framework dogmatism to pragmatism

TOGAF phases and Zachman cells have their value—but in a world of continuous transformation, EA methods must be applied situationally and pragmatically. No framework fits every situation unchanged.

4) From silo guardian to bridge builder

The EA must actively build bridges: between IT and business, between strategic planning and operational execution, between innovation and governance. Stay in your silo—and you lose relevance.

What the EA needs to avoid freezing up

Technical competence still matters

Even as the EA becomes more strategic, they must understand what’s happening technically—AI, cloud-native architectures, API ecosystems, data platforms. Credibility comes from competence, not from titles.

Communication is a core skill

The best architecture plan is useless if no one understands it. EAs must learn to explain complex things simply—at board level and in engineering teams alike.

Resilience and ambiguity tolerance

Transformation means uncertainty. No complete picture, no final answers—just the ability to remain effective anyway and move forward iteratively.

Networking and visibility

Enterprise architecture doesn’t happen in a back room. The EA must be visible, present, and connected—across projects, committees, and communities of practice. If you’re not heard, you’ll be bypassed.

The “off script” role: the EA as Data Migration Lead

No topic in S/4HANA projects is underestimated as consistently as data migration. It’s not a technical side quest—it’s the foundation the entire new world stands on. And that’s exactly why it lands with the EA: because it’s critical, messy, and “kind of technical.” The implicit assumption being, of course, that the EA can simply absorb a full-scale data migration on top of their existing responsibilities—no additional headcount, no external support, no problem. Spoiler: no. For a while, he'll have to unlearn what has become second nature.

By doing so, the EA deliberately steps out of the classic role. They’re no longer the architect at the drawing board, but the Data Migration Lead—archaeologist, diplomat, and crisis manager all in one. This role shift isn’t a failure. It’s a strategic investment in a skill set that most EAs only ever talk about.

What challenges the EA here

The migration topics are multifaceted—and each is its own discipline:

  • Business Partner: customers from Fxxxxx, Ixxxxx, and Nxxxxxxx must be consolidated into a central SAP Business Partner—without duplicates, with clear role logic (customer, supplier, prospect) and consistent master data.
  • Article & material master: color codes, product groups, season flags, and size logic from the source systems must be mapped to a unified S/4HANA data model. Every wrong mapping hits inventory, logistics, and reporting immediately.
  • Open orders: purchase orders and sales orders that span the go-live cutover date must be migrated completely and correctly—no order can be lost.
  • Inventory: the hardest exercise. Physical stock and migrated values must match exactly. Phantom stock and unexplained variances always show up precisely when you need them least. And the quality bar is brutal: inventory values worth several million euros are transferred into the new system. Any deviation hits the balance sheet—and inevitably triggers the auditor. They’ll ask politely—once. After that, the tone tends to change.

What the EA gains from it

This role shift isn’t a detour—it’s an investment in a competence profile no textbook can teach:

  • Deep business understanding: if you know how data really flows, you understand the company at a level no org chart or process model can capture.
  • Stakeholder trust: the EA who has worked in the engine room is taken seriously by business and leadership alike—they’ve proven they can deliver.
  • Architectural credibility: post-migration decisions on data governance, master data management, and system integration are no longer theory—they’re lived experience.
  • Career relevance: data-migration expertise is scarce and in demand. Anyone who has led an S/4HANA migration becomes a highly relevant leader for future transformations—internally and externally.
  • A foundation for real refactoring: after go-live, the EA knows exactly where the technical debt is, which compromises were made, and where cleanup must start. That makes them the best architect for the next phase.

LeanIX: the quiet hero nobody misses—until it’s gone

If you’ve lived through an S/4HANA transformation, you know this moment: someone asks, “Which systems are actually connected to Nxxxxxxx?” or “Who is the business owner of the integration interface to Fxxxxx?” And suddenly everyone looks around.

That’s when LeanIX’s relevance goes through the roof.

LeanIX is the EA’s central tool for application portfolio management and documenting the IT landscape. In calm times, many see it as a nice-to-have—a maintenance tool you “should update at some point.” Keeping LeanIX current when everything is quiet? “We’ll do it after the project.” The project lasts five years. Welcome to the vicious cycle. In a transformation, it turns into critical infrastructure.

Why LeanIX becomes non-negotiable in transformation

  • Make dependencies visible: which applications depend on the legacy systems being decommissioned? Which interfaces must be migrated—and which can be shut down? Without maintained LeanIX, these are assumptions, not facts.
  • Steer the coexistence phase: while old and new systems run in parallel, LeanIX can be the only source that keeps an overview of the landscape state—if it’s kept up to date.
  • Justify migration decisions: which application gets replaced, which stays? Which is technically outdated, which still has strategic relevance? LeanIX provides the data basis for those decisions.
  • Communicate with stakeholders: a maintained LeanIX portfolio replaces endless PowerPoint decks. It makes transformation progress visible—for management and project teams alike.

The bitter paradox

LeanIX is often valued only when the data is missing or out of date: when, during the cutover crunch, nobody can say which system is still writing data into Nxxxxxxx. When the interface documentation dates back to 2016—and reality has long since moved on.

The EA is often the only person who knows why a decision was made years ago—and is politely blamed for it today. That knowledge lives in no ticket, no Confluence page, and no LeanIX record. It sits in their head. If you don’t trust the EA, you’re not really trusting the long-term sustainability of your own IT landscape.

The EA who maintained LeanIX consistently before the transformation is the calmest person in the room in that moment. The EA who didn’t is the one who spends the next weeks reconstructing the landscape through interviews and manual analysis—under time pressure.

Good LeanIX maintenance isn’t bureaucracy. It’s the insurance you never miss—until you need it.

Transformation is also a chance to establish LeanIX permanently: not as “the EA team’s job”, but as a living tool owned by the entire IT organization.

Conclusion: more than an architect

Anyone who lives through this kind of transformation doesn’t come back the same. The EA has worked in the engine room as a Data Migration Lead, defended LeanIX under pressure, mediated across business units, and filled roles that were never in their job description. They outgrew themselves—usually when they had no other choice.

The most valuable skills they take away don’t initially sound like enterprise architecture: data quality, stakeholder diplomacy, operational crisis-readiness, systems thinking under pressure. But that’s exactly what separates a great EA from a good one.

These capabilities aren’t side activities—they’re the multiplier for everything the EA wants to deliver strategically. If you don’t know how data really flows, you design architectures in a vacuum. If you don’t have credibility in day-to-day operations, you won’t be heard strategically.

Your target architecture is beautiful. But reality will still ruin it—slowly, over time and within budget. And the project even has the board’s full support for these unsustainable decisions.

And LeanIX? Next time, they’ll update it first—not because someone demands it, but because they know what happens when nobody does.

The transformation wasn’t a detour. It was the shortest path to becoming a better architect.

In the end, nobody got exactly what they imagined. Everyone still emerged stronger—but everyone walks out with something rare: the shared credibility of having steered the company through the messy part — together, and often well outside their job descriptions.

That's transformation!

entry.title: WPT Is Not Just Configuration — It Is Warehouse Movement Intelligence
entry.content:
Warehouse Process Type Is Not Just Configuration — It Is the Execution Intelligence Behind Every Warehouse Task

Manyat Thinking: Simplicity is the Ultimate Sophistication

Warehouse Process Type Is Not Just Configuration — It Is the Execution Intelligence Behind Every Warehouse Task

Warehouse Process Type Is More Than Configuration: A Different Way to Think About SAP EWM

Introduction

Whenever we learn SAP EWM, Warehouse Process Type (WPT) is usually introduced with a familiar definition:

"A configuration object used to control Warehouse Task creation and execution."

There is nothing technically wrong with this explanation. However, one question always stayed in my mind:

If WPT is only a configuration object, why does almost every warehouse movement depend on it?

That question completely changed the way I think about Warehouse Process Types. Today, I no longer see WPT as just another configuration object. Instead, I see it as the point where a warehouse movement begins to receive its execution behavior.

A Warehouse Task Can Execute… But Can It Decide?

A Warehouse Task tells an operator what to move, where to move it, and how much inventory should be moved.

However, can a Warehouse Task decide whether that movement represents Putaway, Picking, Replenishment, or an Internal Movement?

The answer is no.

That decision already exists before the Warehouse Task is created. The Warehouse Task simply executes the movement, while the Warehouse Process Type determines the business nature of that execution.

That is the difference.

Every Movement Looks Similar Until Business Gets Involved

From a purely technical perspective, every Warehouse Task moves inventory from one place to another.

From a business perspective, however, every movement serves a completely different objective.

A Putaway movement organizes warehouse storage.

A Picking movement fulfills customer demand.

A Replenishment movement protects the picking process.

An Internal Movement improves warehouse operations.

Although the physical movement may appear identical, the business purpose behind each movement is completely different. That business purpose is what changes the expected behavior of the movement, and this is where Warehouse Process Type becomes important.

Think About a Chess Game

The analogy that helped me understand this concept came from chess.

Every chess piece moves across the same board, yet nobody expects a Pawn to behave like a Queen. The board remains the same and the destination may even be identical, but the movement behavior is fundamentally different.

Warehouses operate in a very similar way.

Every Warehouse Task moves inventory, but a Putaway task should never behave like a Picking task. Likewise, a Replenishment task should never behave like an Internal Movement.

The inventory may be identical. The operator may be identical. Even the forklift may be identical.

What changes is the expected behavior of the movement.

That behavior begins with the Warehouse Process Type.

Does WPT Work Alone?

No.

This is one of the biggest misunderstandings people often have when discussing Warehouse Process Types.

I am not suggesting that WPT replaces Storage Type Search, Queue Determination, WOCR, POSC, Wave Management, or any other SAP EWM control object. Every one of these components has its own responsibility within warehouse execution.

However, before SAP EWM can apply all of those controls, the system must first understand one fundamental question:

What kind of warehouse movement is this?

That execution identity originates from the Warehouse Process Type.

The remaining EWM components then refine, optimize, and control the execution of that movement.

Why Should Business Care?

Many warehouse problems are solved by introducing additional configuration.

Sometimes, however, the actual issue is much simpler.

The movement behavior itself was never designed correctly.

A well-designed Warehouse Process Type contributes to cleaner execution, greater operational consistency, easier troubleshooting, improved resource utilization, and lower operational confusion.

The objective is not to create more Warehouse Tasks.

The objective is to create better warehouse behavior.

WPT Also Tells a Story

One interesting observation is that Warehouse Process Types do more than control execution.

They also reveal warehouse behavior.

For example, frequent Emergency Replenishment often indicates weaknesses in inventory planning.

An unusually high number of Internal Movements may suggest poor slotting decisions or warehouse imbalance.

Repeated exception tasks frequently expose unstable business processes.

Warehouse Tasks explain what happened.

Warehouse Process Types often help explain why it keeps happening.

This is where operational visibility begins to generate business value.

Looking Ahead

Warehouses continue to evolve through Robotics, Automation, MFS, and Artificial Intelligence.

All of these technologies are capable of moving inventory.

However, moving inventory is not the difficult part.

Understanding how each movement should behave is.

Automation without well-defined movement behavior simply allows mistakes to happen faster.

Good warehouse execution begins long before a forklift starts moving.

It begins when warehouse movements are designed correctly.

Conclusion

Warehouse Tasks move inventory, but movement alone never creates an efficient warehouse.

Efficient warehouses depend on predictable movement behavior.

Storage Types, Queues, WOCR, POSC, and other SAP EWM components all contribute to warehouse execution. However, before those controls can be applied, Warehouse Process Type establishes the execution identity of the movement.

That is why I no longer see WPT as just another configuration object.

I see it as the point where warehouse movement begins to receive its execution behavior.

Manyat Thinking

Warehouse Tasks execute movement.

Warehouse Process Types define movement behavior.

The goal is not to move inventory faster.

The goal is to ensure every movement behaves correctly before execution begins.

— Rahul Kumar

entry.title: Why Doesn't My Asset Reach Zero Net Book Value at the End of Its Useful Life?
entry.content:
If you have ever reached the end of an asset's useful life and found a remaining Net Book Value (NBV) on the books — even though the asset should be fully depreciated — you are not alone. This is one of the most common sources of confusion in SAP Asset Accounting, and the good news is: the system is not wrong. There is a logical reason behind this behavior.

This post explains why it happens, walks through a concrete example with numbers, and outlines your options to address it.

The Setup: Depreciation from APC and Useful Life

The scenario described here applies when your depreciation key is configured to calculate depreciation based on APC (Acquisition and Production Costs) and useful life. This is a very common and straightforward setup:

Annual Depreciation = APC ÷ Useful Life

With this method, each acquisition amount is depreciated in equal instalments over the full useful life of the asset, starting from the ordinary depreciation start date of that specific acquisition.

That last part is the key.

Step 1: The Simple Case — One Acquisition, No Surprises

Let us start with a clean example. You capitalise an asset with an APC of 3,000 and a useful life of 3 years.

Year APC Used Depreciation Cumulative Dep. NBV

Y13,0001,0001,0002,000
Y23,0001,0002,0001,000
Y33,0001,0003,0000

Simple. At the end of year 3, the NBV is exactly zero. The asset is fully depreciated.

Step 2: A Subsequent Acquisition — Where the Residual Appears

Now suppose that in Year 2, you post an additional acquisition of 3,000 to the same asset. The useful life remains 3 years and you are not extending it.

The system now calculates depreciation independently for each acquisition posting, each starting from its own depreciation start date:

Original acquisition (starts Year 1):

Year Depreciation

Y13,000 ÷ 3 = 1,000
Y23,000 ÷ 3 = 1,000
Y33,000 ÷ 3 = 1,000
Total3,000 — fully depreciated ✓

Second acquisition (starts Year 2):

Year Depreciation

Y1— (not yet capitalised)
Y23,000 ÷ 3 = 1,000
Y33,000 ÷ 3 = 1,000
Total2,000 — only 2 of 3 years depreciated ✗

Combined asset view:

Year Total Depreciation Cumulative NBV

Y11,0001,0005,000
Y22,0003,0003,000
Y32,0005,0001,000

At the end of Year 3 (end of useful life), 1,000 remains on the asset. The system did not make a mistake. The second acquisition simply did not have enough time to be fully depreciated within the original useful life.

A Real Example from the System

The table below mirrors a real asset with an APC of 22,460.02 acquired mid-year in FY2021, depreciated at 20% per year (5-year useful life) alongside an original acquisition from FY2020:

Fiscal Year Annual Dep. (2nd Acq.) Factor Depreciation Posted

FY202122,460.02 × 20%0.83333,743.34
FY202222,460.02 × 20%1.00004,492.00
FY202322,460.02 × 20%1.00004,492.00
FY202422,460.02 × 20%1.00004,492.00
FY202522,460.02 × 20%0.41671,871.67
Total depreciated  19,091.01
Remaining NBV  3,369.00

The original acquisition (FY2020) is fully depreciated. The second acquisition, posted mid-FY2021, is not — because the useful life end date arrived before it had completed its own 5-year depreciation cycle.

"Can I Just Extend the Useful Life?"

This is a natural first thought, but unfortunately it does not solve the problem.

Here is why: the residual NBV comes from the fact that the second acquisition started depreciating later than the asset's ordinary depreciation start date. Extending the useful life shifts the end date forward — but the second acquisition will still be behind by the same gap. There will always be a residual, regardless of how many years you add.

Try it yourself with the 3,000 + 3,000 example above, extending to 4 years:

  • Original acquisition: fully depreciated in Y3, nothing to depreciate in Y4.
  • Second acquisition (starts Y2): depreciated in Y2, Y3, Y4 — still only 3 years out of 4 → residual of 1,000.

The gap does not close.

What Are Your Options?

Option 1: Add a New Depreciation Phase After the End of Useful Life

You can configure a new depreciation phase that activates after the useful life ends. This phase immediately depreciates the remaining NBV in full (sometimes called a "catch-up" or "residual" phase).

Pros: Clean zero NBV. No changes to current depreciation logic.
Cons: A potentially significant depreciation posting occurs after the planned useful life end, which may impact the P&L in that period.

Option 2: Switch to NBV-Based Depreciation with Remaining Useful Life

Instead of calculating depreciation from APC ÷ Useful Life, switch your depreciation key to calculate from Net Book Value ÷ Remaining Useful Life.

With this method, each period's depreciation is recalculated on the current NBV distributed over the remaining periods — so subsequent acquisitions are automatically absorbed into the ongoing depreciation, and the asset naturally converges to zero (or to scrap value) by the end of useful life.

Pros: No residual at end of useful life. Smooth, self-correcting calculation.
Cons: Depreciation amounts are not constant (they fluctuate with acquisitions and write-downs), which may not align with your accounting or reporting preferences.

Summary

Scenario Reaches Zero NBV at End of Useful Life?

Single acquisition, APC ÷ Useful LifeYes
Subsequent acquisition, APC ÷ Useful LifeNo — residual remains
Subsequent acquisition + extended useful lifeNo — residual still remains
NBV ÷ Remaining Useful Life methodYes
APC ÷ Useful Life + catch-up phaseYes (after useful life end)

The right approach depends on your company's accounting policies and reporting requirements. The system offers both paths — the decision belongs to you.

Final Thought

The behavior described in this post is standard and expected in SAP Asset Accounting. Understanding the calculation logic behind your depreciation key is essential to predict how subsequent acquisitions will behave over time.

If you are unsure which option is right for your situation, consider engaging your SAP consultant or implementation partner to evaluate the impact on your chart of depreciation before making any changes.

Have questions or a different scenario? Drop a comment below.
